In the immediate historical context of Isaiah 14, these words are directed toward the King of Babylon, a man whose heart became so bloated with power that he viewed himself as divine. However, throughout church history, many faithful teachers have recognized that behind this earthly tyrant lies the spiritual reality of the fall of Satan. This passage captures the essence of the first sin, which was not a mistake of the mind but a corruption of the will. To be like the Most High is to crave God's incommunicable attributes, such as His absolute sovereignty and His right to be the center of all things.

This prideful ambition is characterized by three specific desires:

First, it is a desire for independence. God alone is self-existent and depends on no one, a reality theologians call His aseity. By saying "I will make myself like the Most High," the rebel attempts to declare independence from God’s rule and providence.

Second, it is a desire for worship. The Most High is the only proper object of the universe's adoration. This rebellion was an attempt to divert the stream of praise away from the Creator and toward the creature.

Third, it is a desire for authority. It is an attempt to sit upon the mount of assembly and exercise a rule that is not derived from or accountable to God. The Bible warns that this is the very snare that led to the devil’s condemnation, as noted in 1 Timothy 3:6.

We see the echo of this ancient rebellion in the Garden of Eden, when the serpent tempted our first parents by promising, "you will be like God" (Genesis 3:5). This same impulse lives in every human heart that seeks to define truth and morality apart from God's Word. It is the tragic irony of sin that by trying to be "like God" on our own terms, we lose the true image of God and fall into ruin.

In beautiful contrast to this path of pride, the Lord Jesus Christ, who is truly God, did not cling to His rights but emptied Himself and took the form of a servant (Philippians 2:5-8). While the rebel sought to ascend above the clouds to seize a throne, Christ descended to the earth and eventually to a cross to save those who had followed the rebel’s path. 

Because of our own pride and sin, we are naturally separated from the Holy God and deserve His just judgment. Yet, the Gospel brings good news: Jesus Christ lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved, rising again on the third day to break the power of sin and death. He is the only way to be reconciled to the Father (John 14:6; Acts 4:12). You can turn to God now, repent of your pride and sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and new life.

A practical step for you this week is to spend time in quiet reflection on Psalm 131. Ask the Holy Spirit to reveal areas where you may be seeking "great matters" or "things too wonderful" for you (Psalm 131:1), and practice the spiritual discipline of submitting your plans and your identity entirely to the lordship of Christ.

Isaiah 14:12-15; Ezekiel 28:11-17; 1 Timothy 3:6; Philippians 2:5-11; Proverbs 16:18

question: What does the Bible say about the hornet?
answer:
The Bible presents the hornet as a tool of God’s divine sovereignty used to clear the way for His people by driving out their enemies. In the accounts of the Exodus and the subsequent conquest of Canaan, the hornet is specifically mentioned as a means of divine judgment that caused panic and forced the pagan nations to flee.

In Exodus 23:27-28, the Lord promises, "I will send My terror before you and throw into confusion every nation you encounter. I will make all your enemies turn their backs and run. I will send the hornet before you to drive the Hivites, Canaanites, and Hittites out of your way." This theme is repeated as Moses prepares the people for the trials ahead in Deuteronomy 7:20, stating, "Moreover, the LORD your God will send the hornet against them until the survivors who hide from you have perished." Finally, after the conquest was underway, Joshua reminded the people in Joshua 24:12 that it was God who had gone before them: "I sent the hornet ahead of you, and it drove out those two kings of the Amorites before you. It was not by your sword or bow."

Whether these verses refer to literal swarms of stinging insects or serve as a vivid metaphor for the terror of God that paralyzed the hearts of the inhabitants of Canaan, the message is one of total dependence on God’s power rather than human might. This highlights the theological reality that God is the Master of His creation and can use any part of it to fulfill His covenant promises.

For the believer today, this provides great comfort. We often find ourselves staring at obstacles that seem like fortified cities, and we are tempted to believe the outcome depends solely on our own sword or bow. However, these passages remind us that the battle belongs to the Lord. He is able to move in ways we cannot see and through means we might never expect to secure our ultimate good and His glory.

As an actionable step in your walk with Christ, I encourage you to identify one specific area of anxiety or opposition you are facing. Instead of relying only on your own strength, bring it before the Lord in prayer. Ask Him to go before you and to work in ways that only He can, trusting that He who began a good work in you will carry it on to completion. Philippians 1:6.

If you are searching for peace in the midst of life’s battles, know that the greatest victory has already been won. Our sin separated us from a holy God, but in His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, defeating death once and for all. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

question: What were the key events in the life of Jesus Christ?
answer:
The life of Jesus Christ is the central hinge of human history, encompassing His miraculous birth, His perfect life of ministry, His sacrificial death, and His glorious resurrection and ascension. These events are not merely religious stories, but historical facts that fulfill ancient prophecies and provide the only way for humanity to be reconciled to a holy God.

**The Incarnation and Birth**
Jesus Christ, who is eternally God, took on human nature through a miraculous conception by the Holy Spirit and was born of the Virgin Mary in Bethlehem. This event, known as the Incarnation, demonstrates the hypostatic union, where Jesus is truly God and truly man. Matthew 1:18-25; Luke 2:1-7.

**Baptism and Temptation**
At the beginning of His public ministry, Jesus was baptized by John the Baptist to fulfill all righteousness, receiving the audible approval of the Father and the visible descending of the Holy Spirit. Immediately following this, He was led into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil, where He remained sinless and victorious, proving His qualifications as our perfect High Priest. Matthew 3:13-17; Matthew 4:1-11; Hebrews 4:15.

**Public Ministry and Miracles**
For approximately three years, Jesus traveled throughout Israel proclaiming the Gospel of the Kingdom, calling people to repentance and faith. He performed many signs and wonders, such as healing the sick and raising the dead, which confirmed His divine authority and identity as the promised Messiah. Mark 1:14-15; John 2:11; John 20:30-31.

**The Transfiguration**
On a high mountain, Jesus’ divine glory was briefly unveiled before Peter, James, and John. This event confirmed His supremacy over the Law and the Prophets and prepared His disciples for the suffering He was about to endure. Matthew 17:1-8.

**The Crucifixion and Atonement**
Jesus was arrested, falsely accused, and sentenced to death by crucifixion under Pontius Pilate. On the cross, He suffered the real wrath of God against sin as a penal substitutionary atonement, meaning He took the punishment that we deserved so that we might be forgiven. John 19:16-30; Romans 5:8; 2 Corinthians 5:21.

**The Resurrection**
On the third day after His burial, Jesus rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. This historical event is the foundation of the Christian faith, confirming that His sacrifice was accepted by the Father and that all who trust in Him will also be raised to eternal life. Luke 24:1-7; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4; 1 Corinthians 15:20.

**The Ascension and Promised Return**
After appearing to many witnesses, Jesus ascended into heaven to sit at the right hand of God the Father, where He currently intercedes for His people as our Advocate. He promised to return personally and bodily to judge the living and the dead and to make all things new. Acts 1:9-11; Hebrews 7:25; Revelation 21:1-5.

In light of who Jesus is and what He has done, I encourage you to look away from your own efforts and fix your eyes on Him. When you feel the weight of your own failures or the brokenness of this world, remember that Christ has lived the perfect life you could not live and died the death you deserved to die. 

God is holy and just, and our sin separates us from Him. Yet, because of His great love, He sent His Son to be the Savior. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Is there anything currently holding you back from placing your full trust in Him?

question: Do Christians need a day of rest?
answer:
Yes, Christians certainly need a day of rest, as God has designed the human heart and body to function within a rhythm of labor and repose. This need is rooted in the very fabric of creation, where God Himself rested on the seventh day, not because He was weary, but to set a pattern for us and to hallow a specific time for communion with Him (Genesis 2:2-3).

The principle of a day of rest is most clearly seen in the Fourth Commandment, where God instructs His people to remember the Sabbath day and keep it holy (Exodus 20:8-11). For the Christian, this rest is both a physical necessity and a spiritual mercy. While the legalistic requirements of the Mosaic Law were fulfilled in Christ, the underlying moral principle remains: we are creatures who depend on our Creator, and we must regularly set aside our worldly toil to find our satisfaction in Him.

In the history of the church, there have been different views on how this is practiced. Some believers, following a Sabbatarian conviction, see the first day of the week, the Lord’s Day, as the "Christian Sabbath" to be kept holy in honor of Christ’s resurrection (Acts 20:7; 1 Corinthians 16:2). Others emphasize Christian liberty, noting that while every day is lived for God, the specific observation of a day is a matter of personal conscience before the Lord (Romans 14:5-6; Colossians 2:16-17). Regardless of the specific theological framework one holds, the wisdom of a dedicated day for corporate worship and physical restoration is a grace we should not despise.

Jesus famously taught that the Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath (Mark 2:27). He did not come to abolish the blessing of rest but to show that He is the true Lord of rest. Our physical rest once a week serves as a beautiful pointer to the greater spiritual rest we find only in the Gospel. We no longer labor to earn God’s favor through our works, but we rest in the finished work of Jesus Christ on the cross.

If you find yourself overwhelmed by the demands of life, remember that true soul rest begins by coming to Jesus. He says, "Come to Me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest" (Matthew 11:28). This invitation is for you today. If you have not yet trusted in Him, the greatest rest you can experience is the forgiveness of your sins and reconciliation with God through faith in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you.

As a practical step for your spiritual health, I encourage you to look at your weekly calendar and intentionally set aside the Lord’s Day for worship with a local church and for cessation from your normal employment. Use this time for prayer, reading the Word, and fellowship. What is one specific task you can set aside this coming Sunday to more fully enjoy God’s gift of rest?

question: How can I be a good Christian?
answer:
To be a "good" Christian is fundamentally to abide in Jesus Christ, recognizing that any spiritual fruit in your life is a result of His grace working through you (John 15:4-5). It begins not with your own moral effort or a list of rules, but with a heart transformed by the Gospel, leading to a life of joyful obedience and love for God and others.

The Bible teaches that we have all sinned and fall short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23). Because God is perfectly holy and just, our sin separates us from Him and deserves His righteous judgment. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live the perfect life we could not live. He died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the full penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord over all.

A person is justified (declared righteous) by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by their own works (Ephesians 2:8-9). To be a Christian is to stop trusting in your own "goodness" and to trust entirely in the finished work of Jesus. If you have not yet done so, you can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

Once you have placed your faith in Christ, you enter the lifelong process of sanctification. This is the work of the Holy Spirit within you, conforming you to the image of Jesus. Being a "good" Christian in this sense means walking in the Spirit and growing in holiness, not to earn God's love, but because you are already loved by Him.

As an actionable next step, I encourage you to commit yourself to the "means of grace" that God has provided for your growth. This includes a daily rhythm of reading the Bible to know God's mind, speaking to Him in prayer, and joining a local, Bible-preaching church where you can receive the Word and fellowship with other believers (Acts 2:42; Hebrews 10:24-25). Genuine faith always expresses itself in love and service to others, reflecting the light of Christ to a world in need (Matthew 5:16).

question: What does the Bible say about lawsuits / suing?
answer:
The Bible teaches that Christians should avoid taking one another to secular courts to resolve disputes, as this harms the witness of the Gospel and suggests that the wisdom of the world is superior to the wisdom found within the body of Christ. The primary instruction on this matter is found in the New Testament, where the emphasis is placed on the church’s internal ability to settle grievances through the wisdom of the Holy Spirit.

In 1 Corinthians 6:1–6, the Apostle Paul expresses holy astonishment that believers would bring their disagreements before "the unrighteous" rather than before the saints. He reminds the church that because believers will one day judge the world and even angels, they should be more than capable of handling "trivial cases" in this life. To seek a secular judge over a Christian brother or sister is described as a defeat for the church, as it exposes internal division to a watching world.

The Scriptures go even further, challenging the believer’s heart and priorities. Paul asks in 1 Corinthians 6:7, "Why not rather be wronged? Why not rather be defrauded?" This suggests that it is better to suffer personal or financial loss than to damage the reputation of Jesus Christ by engaging in public legal warfare with a fellow believer. This reflects the character of our Lord, who committed Himself to Him who judges righteously rather than demanding His rights (1 Peter 2:23).

While the prohibition against lawsuits is explicit regarding "brother against brother," the Bible does recognize that God has established civil authorities to maintain order and execute justice in society (Romans 13:1–4). In dealings with those outside the faith, a Christian may at times need to interact with the legal system, yet even then, we are called to live peaceably with all men as far as it depends on us (Romans 12:18).

This teaching rests on the principle of **ecclesiastical authority**, which is the God-given responsibility of the local church to provide oversight and wise counsel to its members. When we bypass the church for the courtroom, we often ignore the heart of the matter, which is usually a breach of love or a struggle with greed.

If you find yourself in a conflict with a fellow believer, the path forward is found in the words of Jesus. In Matthew 18:15–17, He provides a clear process for reconciliation:
1. Go to the person privately to discuss the matter.
2. If they do not listen, take one or two others along so that every charge may be established by the evidence of two or three witnesses.
3. If the person still refuses to listen, bring the matter to the local church leadership.

Your next actionable step should be to contact your church elders or pastors. Ask them to facilitate a meeting or provide biblical mediation for the dispute. By doing this, you honor the order God has placed in His church and prioritize the unity of the Spirit.

Ultimately, our approach to justice must be shaped by the Gospel. We were all once defendants in the court of God’s holiness, guilty of sin and unable to pay our debt. Yet, God, in His mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take our place. Christ lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy God's justice, and rose bodily from the grave to secure our justification. When we realize how much we have been forgiven in Christ, it changes how we view the debts others owe us. You can trust God with your heart and your resources today, knowing that He is your provider and your final Judge.

question: What is kingdom now teaching?
answer:
Kingdom Now theology is the teaching that Jesus Christ cannot or will not return to the earth until the Church has successfully established His Kingdom by taking dominion over all social, political, and cultural institutions. This perspective, often linked with Dominion Theology, suggests that because Adam lost his authority over the earth at the Fall, it is the modern Church's mandate to reclaim that authority and "Christianize" the world to prepare a place for the Lord's return.

While it is noble to desire that our culture reflects God's righteousness, Kingdom Now teaching often shifts the focus away from the biblical Gospel of the salvation of souls toward a gospel of societal transformation. It places the timing of Christ's return in the hands of human effort rather than in the sovereign hand of God. The Bible, however, describes a world that remains fallen and even grows more difficult for believers as the end approaches (2 Timothy 3:1–5).

Scripture teaches that the Kingdom of God is "already" here in a spiritual sense through the hearts of believers, but it is "not yet" fully realized in a physical or political sense. Jesus told Pilate, "My kingdom is not of this world" (John 18:36). The full consummation of the Kingdom—the renewal of all things and the ending of sin and sorrow—will not be achieved by human activism, but by the personal and bodily return of Jesus Christ Himself (Revelation 21:1–5). 

As believers, our primary calling is the Great Commission, which is to make disciples of all nations by proclaiming the Gospel (Matthew 28:18–20). We serve as salt and light in a dark world, but we do so knowing that we are "sojourners and exiles" (1 Peter 2:11). Our hope is not in our ability to perfect the world, but in the King who has already conquered sin and death through His sacrifice and resurrection.

If you are concerned about the direction of society, remember that God remains supreme and sovereign over all history (Psalm 103:19). I encourage you to read through the Gospel of John this week and notice how Jesus speaks of His authority and the nature of His Kingdom. You can turn to Him even now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died for you and rose again, to give you peace and eternal life.

What holds you back from fully resting in His finished work today?

question: What is The Infinite Way?
answer:
The Infinite Way is a mystical spiritual philosophy founded by Joel S. Goldsmith in 1947 that emphasizes the attainment of a divine consciousness and the belief that God is the only reality. It teaches that through meditation and silence, an individual can realize their oneness with God, which supposedly results in healing, harmony, and the vanishing of "illusions" such as sin, disease, and death.

While the movement often uses biblical terminology, its core teachings stand in direct opposition to the historic Christian faith revealed in the Holy Scriptures. It is vital to understand these distinctions with clarity and love, for the soul's rest depends on the true Christ, not a mystical concept.

The Infinite Way teaches a form of monism, suggesting that God is the only substance and that everything else, including human suffering and sin, is an illusion of the human mind. However, the Bible teaches that God is the personal Creator who is distinct from His creation as seen in Genesis 1:1 and Nehemiah 9:6. Sin is not an illusion or a mistake of the mind; it is a real, rebellious violation of God's holy law that separates us from Him, as stated in Romans 3:23 and Isaiah 59:2.

Furthermore, this movement reframes "Christ" as a state of consciousness that anyone can achieve. In contrast, the Word of God declares that Jesus Christ is the unique, eternal Son of God, truly God and truly man, who took on flesh to save us. He is not a "level of awareness" but a living Person, the only Mediator between God and men according to 1 Timothy 2:5 and John 1:1, John 1:14.

The Infinite Way seeks peace through the realization of one's own divinity, but true peace only comes through the Prince of Peace. We are not divine beings who have forgotten our nature; we are lost sheep in need of a Shepherd. Our problem is not ignorance, but guilt before a holy God, and our only hope is the finished work of Jesus Christ on the cross.

The true Gospel is this: God is holy and we are sinful. Out of His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day as described in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. He ascended to heaven and is Lord over all. Salvation is not found in changing our consciousness, but in being born again by the Holy Spirit through faith in Jesus.

You may wonder if looking within is the path to finding God, but the heart is often deceptive as noted in Jeremiah 17:9. I invite you to look outward and upward to the historical Jesus. Turn from your own efforts and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive total forgiveness and eternal life.

A helpful next step would be to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. As you read, notice how Jesus claims a unique authority that no other human can possess. You might also find it helpful to speak with a local pastor who can guide you through the Scriptures. What is it about this mystical approach that draws your interest?

question: Does God have favorites?
answer:
God is impartial in His justice and does not show favoritism based on outward appearance, worldly status, or human merit. Scripture plainly declares that there is no partiality with God (Romans 2:11; Acts 10:34-35).

This means that God does not look at a person’s wealth, ethnicity, or social standing to determine their worth or their standing before His throne. He is the righteous Judge who treats all people according to the truth of His holy standard. However, while God is impartial in His justice, He is sovereignly particular in His mercy and grace. We see this in the doctrine of sovereign election, where God chooses to set His special, redeeming love upon specific people according to His own good pleasure and purpose, rather than anything they have done to deserve it (Ephesians 1:4-6; Romans 9:11-16).

For example, God chose the nation of Israel not because they were more numerous or righteous than other nations, but simply because He loved them and chose to keep His oath (Deuteronomy 7:7-8). In the same way, for those who are in Christ, God has demonstrated a "favor" that is rooted entirely in His grace. This is not favoritism in the worldly sense of being "impressed" by someone, but a fatherly, elective love that adopts sinners into His family.

You may wonder if a God who chooses in this way is unfair or exclusive. It is helpful to remember that because of our sin, none of us deserve God's favor. We have all rebelled against our Creator and earned His righteous judgment (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). If God were only "fair," we would all remain separated from Him forever. The beauty of the Gospel is that God, in His infinite kindness, intervened to save.

God is holy and requires perfect righteousness. Because we are sinful and unable to save ourselves, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take on human nature. Jesus lived a perfect life of obedience, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death, and He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your walk with Him, I encourage you to read through the first chapter of Ephesians this week. Reflect on the riches of being "blessed in the Heavenly realms" and what it means to be adopted as His child. Additionally, find a local, Bible-teaching church where you can grow in the understanding of God's character alongside other believers.

Seek the Lord in prayer, asking Him to reveal the depth of His love to you. You might speak to Him simply, acknowledging your need for His grace and thanking Him for the mercy found in His Son. James 4:8 promises that if you draw near to God, He will draw near to you.

question: How can I live a holy life?
answer:
Living a holy life is the process of being set apart for God’s glory and transformed into the image of His Son, Jesus Christ, by the power of the Holy Spirit. This journey, often called progressive sanctification, is not a human effort to earn God’s love, but a joyful response to the grace He has already poured out on us through the Gospel (1 Peter 1:15-16; Romans 12:1).

To understand how to live a holy life, we must first distinguish between justification and sanctification. In justification, God declares you righteous and holy once and for all because of the perfect obedience and sacrificial death of Jesus Christ (Romans 5:1). You cannot add to this standing. Sanctification, however, is the lifelong work where the Spirit of God weakens the power of sin in your life and strengthens your desire for righteousness (1 Thessalonians 4:3; Philippians 2:12-13).

To grow in holiness, you should consistently walk in the means of grace God has provided. This begins with immersing yourself in the Word of God, as Jesus prayed, "Sanctify them by the truth; Your word is truth" (John 17:17). It involves a life of prayer, continually asking for the Spirit's help to mortify, or put to death, the sinful deeds of the body (Romans 8:13). Finally, do not neglect the local church, where you receive the preaching of the Word and the accountability of fellow believers who spur you on toward love and good deeds (Hebrews 10:24-25).

If you find yourself struggling with the weight of your own failures, remember that holiness starts with a heart transformed by Christ. God is perfectly holy, and our sin separates us from Him. Yet, out of His immense love, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived the perfect life we could not live and died on the cross as a substitute to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death, and is now exalted as Lord. Salvation and the power to live a new life are gifts of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus for forgiveness and the strength to follow Him.

As a practical step this week, I encourage you to begin each day by reading a chapter of the Gospel of John or a Psalm, and ask the Lord specifically for the grace to honor Him in your thoughts and your conversations with others.

What specific area of your life do you feel the greatest struggle in seeking to honor God?

question: What is the sinner's prayer?
answer:
The sinner's prayer is a prayer that an individual offers to God when they desire to repent of their sins and place their trust in Jesus Christ for salvation. It serves as an outward expression of a heart that has come to recognize its own spiritual bankruptcy and its desperate need for the mercy found only in the Lord Jesus Christ.

While there is no specific formula or exact script found in the Bible that one must recite to be saved, the components of such a prayer are deeply biblical. It typically involves acknowledging one's sinfulness, confessing that Jesus Christ is Lord, and believing that He died for our sins and rose again from the dead. The Scripture promises that everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved (Romans 10:13). A beautiful example of this heart posture is seen in the parable of the Pharisee and the tax collector, where the tax collector simply cried out, "God, have mercy on me, a sinner" (Luke 18:13).

It is vital to understand that the prayer itself does not possess a magical power to save. We are not justified by the eloquence of our words or the act of reciting a prayer, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9). Salvation is the work of God the Holy Spirit, who convicts us of sin and grants us the gift of repentance. The prayer is simply the means by which a person lays hold of the promise of the Gospel. Justification is a legal declaration by God where the righteousness of Christ is credited to the believer, and their sins are forgiven because of Christ's substitutionary death on the cross.

The Gospel is the good news that although God is perfectly holy and we have all rebelled against Him, God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that whoever believes in Him should not perish but have eternal life (John 3:16). Jesus Christ, being truly God and truly man, lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved, satisfying the righteous wrath of God against sin. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death.

If you recognize your need for forgiveness, you can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical next step, I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John in the Bible to see the character and work of Jesus more clearly. I also urge you to seek out a local, Bible-teaching church where you can hear the Word of God preached and find fellowship with other believers who can walk alongside you in your journey of faith.

question: How can a Christian avoid being a doormat for other people?
answer:
A Christian avoids being a "doormat" by recognizing that biblical meekness is not weakness, but rather power under the control of the Holy Spirit. To follow Christ is to live for the "audience of One," which often requires saying "no" to the sinful demands or manipulations of others in order to say "yes" to God.

True humility is not thinking less of yourself, but thinking of yourself less while thinking of God more. When we confuse being "loving" with being "compliant" to every human whim, we risk making an idol out of human approval. The Apostle Paul was clear when he asked, "Am I now seeking the approval of men, or of God? Or am I striving to please men? If I were still trying to please men, I would not be a servant of Christ" (Galatians 1:10). If your desire to be "nice" prevents you from standing for the truth or practicing wise stewardship of your life, you have moved from Christian service into people-pleasing.

Consider the example of our Lord Jesus. He was the perfect model of sacrificial love, yet He was never a pawn for the agendas of others. He frequently withdrew from the crowds to pray when they wanted His miracles (Luke 5:16), He spoke hard truths to those in power (Matthew 23:13-36), and He ultimately laid down His life voluntarily. His sacrifice was an act of sovereign will, not a result of being bullied. We are called to be "wise as serpents and innocent as doves" (Matthew 10:16). This requires the discernment to know when to give of ourselves and when to set a boundary for the sake of the Gospel and our own spiritual health.

Setting boundaries is actually an act of love for your neighbor. If you allow someone to continue in patterns of manipulation, selfishness, or abuse without consequence, you are not helping them; you are enabling their sin. Speaking the truth in love (Ephesians 4:15) sometimes means saying "no" so that the other person might see their need for repentance. A person without self-control is like "a city broken into and left without walls" (Proverbs 25:28). You must maintain the walls of your soul so that you can serve God effectively.

If you find yourself constantly crushed by the expectations of others, it may be because you are seeking from people the validation that only God can give. The Gospel tells us that through the death and resurrection of Jesus Christ, we are fully known and fully loved by the Creator of the universe. When you trust in Christ’s finished work, you are justified by grace through faith (Ephesians 2:8-9). This reality frees you from the need to earn the favor of others. You are no longer a slave to their opinions but a servant of the Most High.

As a practical step in your sanctification, identify one relationship where you have felt pressured to compromise your convictions or your well-being. Pray for the courage to speak the truth clearly and gently to that person this week. Ask a mature believer or an elder in your local church to pray with you and provide accountability as you learn to walk in the strength that God provides.

If you have never placed your trust in Jesus, you may be trying to find your worth in how much you can do for others. But your works cannot save you. God is holy, and we have all sinned against Him, but He sent His Son to live a perfect life and die on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over death. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and new life. What is preventing you from resting in His love today?

question: What does it mean that the workers are few (Matthew 9:37)?
answer:
When Jesus observed that the workers are few, He was pointing to the vast disproportion between the multitude of souls needing the Gospel and the small number of faithful believers actively engaged in sharing it and ministering to them. This statement, found in Matthew 9:37 and Luke 10:2, follows a moment where Jesus looked upon the crowds and was moved with compassion because they were confused and aimless, like sheep without a shepherd.

The harvest describes those in the world who are ripe for hearing the message of salvation. While the spiritual need is immense, the laborers—those who are willing to preach the Word, teach the truth, and serve the broken—remain a small remnant. This highlights that the primary obstacle to the spread of the Gospel is not a lack of interest or a lack of power in the message, but a shortage of dedicated servants willing to go into the field.

In the context of God's sovereignty, this passage teaches us that while God is the Lord of the harvest who ultimately saves, He chooses to use human instruments as the means of grace to gather His people. These workers are not only pastors or missionaries, but all believers who have been justified by grace through faith and are now called to the work of the Great Commission. 

This reality should lead us to a specific pastoral response. Jesus does not tell His disciples to become discouraged by the numbers or to work in their own strength to manufacture a harvest. Instead, He gives a direct command to pray: "Ask the Lord of the harvest, therefore, to send out workers into His harvest field" (Matthew 9:38). Our first labor is the labor of prayer, recognizing that only God can call, equip, and send people into the spiritual fray.

The urgency of this harvest reminds us of the serious condition of the human heart. Because of sin, every person is naturally separated from a holy and just God. However, God in His great love sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to be the perfect Shepherd. Jesus took on human nature, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our transgressions. He was buried and rose bodily from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now the exalted Lord who offers full forgiveness to all who come to Him. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your salvation and new life.

As a practical step in response to this truth, I encourage you to set aside time this week to pray specifically for the workers in your own local church and for the mission fields around the world. Ask the Lord how He might want to use your unique gifts and your daily life to be a worker in the corner of the harvest field where He has placed you. Matthew 9:36; Matthew 9:37; Matthew 9:38; Luke 10:2; Romans 10:14; Romans 10:15.

question: What was the purpose of the Levitical Law?
answer:
The Levitical Law was given by God to the nation of Israel at Mount Sinai to reveal His holy character, define the nature of sin, and point the way to the coming Savior. It served as a divine tutor to show God's people that they could never achieve righteousness through their own efforts, but instead required a perfect substitute to reconcile them to a holy God.

The law functioned in several vital ways for the believer:

First, the law revealed the absolute holiness of God. In Leviticus, God repeatedly tells His people, "Be holy, because I am holy" (Leviticus 11:44; Leviticus 19:2). By giving specific instructions for worship and daily living, God demonstrated that He is set apart from all that is common or unclean. This standard of holiness established a clear boundary between the Creator and His fallen creatures.

Second, the law exposed the reality of human sin and "total depravity," which is the biblical truth that sin has corrupted every part of our nature. Through the law, "every mouth may be silenced and the whole world held accountable to God" (Romans 3:19). The law acts as a mirror, showing us the stains on our souls that we cannot wash away ourselves. As the Apostle Paul wrote, "through the law we become conscious of sin" (Romans 3:20).

Third, the law established the principle of "substitutionary atonement." The complex system of animal sacrifices, particularly the rituals described for the Day of Atonement, taught that the wages of sin is death, but that God would graciously accept the life of a blameless substitute in place of the sinner (Leviticus 16:1-34). These sacrifices were never intended to take away sins permanently, but were "shadows" of the reality to come in Jesus Christ (Hebrews 10:1-4).

Finally, the Levitical Law served as a "typology," or a prophetic picture, of the person and work of Jesus Christ. Every priest, every lamb, and every festival pointed toward the Messiah. The law was our "tutor to lead us to Christ, so that we might be justified by faith" (Galatians 3:24).

Because God is holy and we are sinful, we stand in need of a Mediator. The good news is that Jesus Christ, being truly God and truly man, lived the perfect life that the law demanded and we could not achieve. He then died on the cross as a perfect sacrifice to satisfy the justice of God on our behalf. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is not found in keeping the law, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

If you feel the weight of your own imperfection when looking at God's standards, I invite you to turn from your sin today and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive full forgiveness and eternal life.

To grow in your understanding of how the law and the Gospel relate, I encourage you to read the Book of Galatians this week. As you read, pray that the Holy Spirit would show you the beauty of the freedom we have in Christ, who has fulfilled the law for us.

question: What does the Bible say about confession of sin to a priest?
answer:
The Bible teaches that Jesus Christ is the sole mediator between God and man, and therefore, believers have direct access to God for the confession of sin. While the Old Testament established a human priesthood to mediate through sacrifices, the New Covenant reveals that Jesus has fulfilled and surpassed that role as our eternal High Priest.

The Scriptures are clear that for there is one God and one mediator between God and mankind, the man Christ Jesus (1 Timothy 2:5). Because Christ has offered a single, perfect sacrifice for sins, He has opened the way for us to come boldly to the throne of grace to receive mercy (Hebrews 4:14-16). When we sin, our primary and essential confession is directed to God Himself, who is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness (1 John 1:9).

Within historic Christianity, there are different understandings of how confession should be practiced:

In the Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox traditions, confession to a priest is viewed as a sacrament. This view often points to John 20:23, where Jesus tells the apostles that if they forgive the sins of any, they are forgiven. They believe the priest acts in the person of Christ to provide a formal assurance of God's pardon.

In the Protestant and Reformed tradition, including the Reformed Baptist perspective, we hold to the priesthood of all believers (1 Peter 2:9). This means every Christian has the right and duty to go directly to God through Christ without the need for an earthly intermediary. We understand the passage in John 20:23 as the church’s authority to declare the Gospel, where forgiveness is found. However, Scripture does encourage believers to confess your sins to one another and pray for one another, so that you may be healed (James 5:16). This type of confession is for mutual accountability, support, and the restoration of fellowship within the church body, rather than for the judicial granting of divine forgiveness.

The beauty of the Gospel is that you do not need to wait for a human appointment to find peace with God. God is holy and just, and our sin separates us from Him. Yet, in His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross, bearing the punishment we deserved. He rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation and the full forgiveness of sins are not earned by our works or through religious rituals, but are received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

If you are burdened by guilt today, you can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. He is a tender Savior who welcomes all who come to Him in faith.

As a practical step, I encourage you to set aside time today to speak honestly with God in prayer. Use the words of Psalm 51 as a guide to express your heart to Him. If your conscience remains heavy, consider seeking out a faithful pastor or a mature believer in your local church to pray with you and remind you of the promises of the Gospel.

question: Should Christians go to nightclubs? Is clubbing a sin?
answer:
The Bible calls believers to a life of distinct holiness and joyful devotion to Jesus Christ, which stands in stark contrast to the typical environment and purpose of a nightclub. While the word "nightclub" does not appear in Scripture, the principles governing our conduct, our company, and our witness provide clear guidance that should lead a Christian to avoid the clubbing lifestyle.

God’s standard for His children is clear: "But just as he who called you is holy, so be holy in all you do; for it is written: 'Be holy, because I am holy'" (1 Peter 1:15–16). Holiness means being "set apart" for God’s purposes. The nightclub environment is generally designed to facilitate the "works of the flesh," which include drunkenness, sensuality, and carousing (Galatians 5:19–21). To deliberately walk into a place where the atmosphere is thick with temptation and the celebration of sin is to ignore the command to "make no provision for the flesh, to gratify its desires" (Romans 13:14).

Is clubbing a sin? While standing inside a particular building is not inherently sinful, the activities that define the "clubbing" experience—such as immodest dancing, the pursuit of sexualized attention, and the consumption of alcohol to the point of lost self-control—are contrary to the life of the Spirit. Scripture warns us to "have no fellowship with the fruitless deeds of darkness, but rather expose them" (Ephesians 5:11). A Christian’s body is not their own; it is "a temple of the Holy Spirit" (1 Corinthians 6:19–20). We are called to glorify God with our bodies, not to offer them as instruments of worldly pleasure.

Furthermore, we must consider our witness to a watching world. We are called to be "the salt of the earth" and "the light of the world" (Matthew 5:13–14). If a believer is indistinguishable from the world in their pursuit of entertainment, the power of their testimony is greatly diminished. Love for the world and love for the Father cannot occupy the same heart at the same time: "Do not love the world or anything in the world. If anyone loves the world, love for the Father is not in them" (1 John 2:15).

If you find yourself drawn to the excitement of the club, I encourage you to examine your heart. Are you seeking to fill a void that only Christ can satisfy? True, lasting joy is found in the presence of God, not in the fleeting, hollow lights of a dance floor. We were all once "darkness," but now we are "light in the Lord" (Ephesians 5:8). Jesus Christ lived the perfect life we could not live and died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, rising again to give us a brand-new life. This grace does not just forgive our past; it transforms our current desires.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to evaluate your current social circle and find a local church where you can experience the deep, authentic joy of Christian fellowship. True "nightlife" for a believer is found in the warmth of the community of saints, where we encourage one another toward love and good deeds. Turn to the Lord in prayer today, asking Him to give you a greater hunger for His Word and a deeper satisfaction in His presence than anything the world can offer.

question: Does the statue outside the United Nations have any connection to the beast of the end times?
answer:
The statue you are referring to, titled the Guardian for International Peace and Security, was a temporary installation at the United Nations that many believers have noted bears a striking visual resemblance to the beasts described in the visions of Daniel and the apostle John.

In the biblical accounts, these beasts are not mere metaphors, but represent real earthly powers and a coming global system that opposes God. Daniel 7:4 describes a beast that was like a lion and had the wings of an eagle. Furthermore, Revelation 13:2 describes a beast that was like a leopard, but had feet like those of a bear and a mouth like that of a lion. While the United Nations statue was described by its creators as a fusion of a jaguar and an eagle, the similarity to these scriptural descriptions is naturally provocative to those who study the Word of God.

However, we must exercise biblical wisdom and discernment. Throughout history, many symbols and monuments have appeared to mirror prophetic imagery. While the visual parallels are noteworthy, the Bible teaches that the actual Beast of the end times will be characterized by a specific set of actions: he will demand universal worship, speak blasphemies against the Most High, and exercise authority over every tribe and nation. Revelation 13:5–8. We should be watchful and sober minded, but we must also be careful not to engage in date setting or definitive identifications that Scripture does not explicitly confirm.

Questions regarding the exact timing and identification of end times symbols are considered tertiary matters. Faithful Christians hold various views on how these prophecies will be fulfilled. Some interpret these visions as referring to historical empires like Babylon or Rome, while others look for a future literal fulfillment involving a final world leader. Regardless of the specific interpretation, the central fact remains that God is sovereign over all nations. He brings down one ruler and raises up another, and all earthly powers are ultimately accountable to Him. Psalm 2:1–12; Daniel 4:17.

Dear friend, while it is right to be aware of the signs of the times, our primary focus must remain on the Prince of Peace, Jesus Christ. We live in a world broken by sin and in rebellion against its Creator. God is holy and just, and our sins have separated us from Him. Yet, in His mercy, God sent His Son into the world. Jesus Christ, being truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord of all and will one day return to judge the world and make all things new. Revelation 19:11–16.

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works of our own. Ephesians 2:8–9. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step, I encourage you to commit to reading one chapter of the Gospel of John each day this week. Focus your heart on the character and victory of the Savior, which provides a steady anchor regardless of the shifting shadows of world events. Seek out a local, Bible teaching church where you can grow in fellowship and be strengthened by the means of grace. Romans 10:17; Hebrews 10:24–25.

question: What is the sign of Jonah?
answer:
The sign of Jonah is the death, burial, and bodily resurrection of the Lord Jesus Christ. When the scribes and Pharisees demanded a miraculous sign to prove His authority, Jesus pointed them to the experience of the prophet Jonah as the ultimate prophetic picture of His own mission.

In the Gospel of Matthew 12:39, Jesus says, "An evil and adulterous generation craves a sign, but no sign will be given to it except the sign of the prophet Jonah." He provides the specific interpretation in the following verse, stating that "just as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so the Son of Man will be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth" (Matthew 12:40).

This sign functions in two profound ways. First, it is a matter of historical typology. Jonah was a real man who underwent a miraculous deliverance, which served as a "type" or a foreshadowing of the literal, physical resurrection of Jesus. Just as Jonah emerged from the fish to preach to the Gentiles, Jesus rose from the dead to offer salvation to all nations. This resurrection is the cornerstone of the Christian faith, proving that Jesus is the Son of God and that His sacrifice for sin was accepted by the Father (Romans 1:4).

Second, the sign of Jonah is a call to urgent repentance. Jesus noted that the people of Nineveh repented at the preaching of Jonah, yet someone "greater than Jonah" was standing right in front of the religious leaders (Matthew 12:41). To witness the truth of Christ and remain indifferent is a grave danger. The sign of Jonah leaves us without excuse, for the empty tomb declares that Jesus is Lord of all.

We must remember that God is perfectly holy and righteous, while we have all fallen short through our sin and rebellion. This separation leads to spiritual death, but God, in His great mercy, sent His Son. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose again in victory over the grave.

If you find yourself seeking a sign or wondering about the truth of God, look to the finished work of Christ. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone, not by our own works or efforts. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for the forgiveness of sins and the gift of eternal life.

As a practical next step in your spiritual journey, I encourage you to read the Book of Jonah in the Old Testament followed by the Gospel of Matthew in the New Testament. Reflect on how God’s mercy reaches even the most wayward hearts. What is it that holds you back from fully trusting in the Savior who conquered death for you?

question: What does it mean to walk worthy?
answer:
To walk worthy means to live a life that corresponds to the weight, value, and character of the Gospel of Jesus Christ. It is the practical, outward demonstration of the internal transformation God has already accomplished in a believer by His grace.

The Apostle Paul provides this exhortation in Ephesians 4:1, where he beseeches believers to walk worthy of the calling with which they were called. The term for worthy carries the imagery of a scale, suggesting that our daily conduct should balance with the high dignity of our position as children of God. It is vital to recognize that walking worthy is never the cause of our salvation, but rather the necessary consequence of it. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, but that faith is never alone; it always produces the fruit of a changed life. This process of growing in holiness is known as sanctification.

To walk worthy involves several key biblical markers:

1.  **Humility and Gentleness:** Paul immediately follows his command by calling for all humility and gentleness, with longsuffering, bearing with one another in love, as seen in Ephesians 4:2.
2.  **Fruitfulness:** In Colossians 1:10, walking worthy is described as being fruitful in every good work and increasing in the knowledge of God.
3.  **Holiness:** It involves a separation from the patterns of this world and an imitation of the character of God, who called us into His own kingdom and glory, according to 1 Thessalonians 2:12.

When we fail to walk worthy, we do not lose our standing in Christ, but we do grieve the Holy Spirit and obscure the beauty of the Gospel to a watching world. Walking worthy means that when we sin, we quickly repent and return to the cross, finding our strength once again in the finished work of Jesus.

If you find yourself struggling to live up to this standard, remember that you cannot do it in your own strength. The Gospel is not a message of self-improvement, but of total rescue. God is perfectly holy, and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, as noted in Romans 3:23. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life of "worthy walking" on our behalf. He died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and the power of the Holy Spirit to walk in a new way.

As a practical step in your spiritual growth, I encourage you to sit down this evening and read the first three chapters of Ephesians. Take note of every spiritual blessing God has given you in Christ. Once you have seen the height of your calling, read chapters four through six and ask the Lord in prayer to help you live a life that reflects the greatness of those truths. Seek the accountability of a faithful local church where the Word of God is preached, for we are not called to walk this path alone.

question: What is a divine appointment?
answer:
A divine appointment is a moment sovereignly arranged by God where His providence brings people together to accomplish His specific spiritual purposes. These encounters are not mere coincidences but are the intentional orchestration of the Lord to display His grace, provide guidance, or share the message of salvation.

The theological foundation for this is the **providence** of God, which is His purposeful sovereignty by which He upholds, directs, and governs all creatures and events. We see a beautiful example of this when the Holy Spirit directed Philip to a specific desert road at a specific time to meet an Ethiopian official who was reading the prophet Isaiah but did not understand it (Acts 8:26-31). This was no random meeting, but a bridge built by God so that Philip could explain the good news of Jesus Christ. Likewise, Jesus "had to pass through Samaria," not because of geography alone, but because of a divine appointment with a woman at a well who needed the living water that only He could provide (John 4:4-7).

For the believer, recognizing divine appointments changes how we view the daily schedule. What we often call "interruptions" may actually be the very work God has prepared for us to do. As the Scripture says, "The heart of man plans his way, but the Lord establishes his steps" (Proverbs 16:9). Every person you encounter and every "random" conversation is an opportunity held in the hand of a sovereign God.

As a practical step, I encourage you to begin your day by asking the Lord to give you eyes to see the people He places in your path. When an unexpected conversation arises, pray silently for wisdom and look for a way to speak of the hope found in Christ. 

If you find yourself seeking God today, know that it is no accident. God is holy and supreme, yet we have all sinned and separated ourselves from Him. In His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a sacrificial death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day to victory. He is the only way to the Father (John 14:6). You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What holds you back from trusting Him today?

question: What is the Qur’an?
answer:
The Qur’an is the central religious text of Islam, which Muslims believe to be the verbatim word of God revealed to the prophet Muhammad over a period of approximately twenty three years. It consists of 114 chapters, called surahs, and serves as the foundational authority for Islamic law, theology, and daily conduct.

You may wonder how this book compares to the Bible or why it holds such a profound influence over billions of people who seek structure and moral clarity in their lives. A sincere concern for many is the desire to know which path truly leads to peace with God and how to distinguish between different claims of divine revelation.

The Bible teaches that it alone is the inspired, inerrant, and final Word of God, written by over forty authors over 1,500 years, yet maintaining a perfect unity in its message of redemption through Jesus Christ. 2 Timothy 3:16; 2 Peter 1:20-21. While the Qur’an mentions many biblical figures, it presents a significantly different view of God and the way of salvation. Specifically, it denies the Trinity and the deity of Jesus Christ, viewing Him only as a human prophet. Christians believe, based on the historical and eyewitness testimony of Scripture, that Jesus is Vera Deus, vera homo, truly God and truly man. John 1:1; John 1:14; Colossians 2:9.

Furthermore, the Qur’an teaches that salvation is primarily achieved through a combination of belief and righteous deeds, whereas the Bible declares that human works can never satisfy the perfect justice of a holy God. Romans 3:20. The Gospel is the good news that because we are unable to save ourselves from the penalty of our sin, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross, and rise bodily on the third day. Romans 5:8; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. Through His sacrifice, the wrath of God against sin is satisfied for all who believe.

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own efforts. Ephesians 2:8-9. God’s Word invites you to look to Jesus, the radiance of God’s glory and the exact representation of His being. Hebrews 1:3. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive total forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him as your Lord and Savior today?

question: What would have happened “if the Lord had not been on our side” (Psalm 124:2)?
answer:
If the Lord had not been on our side, the Bible declares that we would have been utterly consumed, overwhelmed, and destroyed by the forces aligned against us. In Psalm 124, King David describes a terrifying reality of what life looks like without the sovereign protection of God. He uses vivid imagery to show that our survival is never a result of our own luck, strength, or wisdom, but is entirely due to the intervening grace of the Creator.

According to the text, several specific things would have happened:

First, we would have been swallowed up alive (Psalm 124:3). David suggests that the anger of our enemies would have been so fierce and sudden that it would have devoured us completely, leaving no trace behind. This speaks to the predatory nature of sin and the spiritual forces of darkness that seek the total ruin of God's people.

Second, we would have been swept away by an unstoppable flood (Psalm 124:4–5). The psalmist describes waters overwhelming the soul and a raging stream rising above our heads. Without the Lord as our anchor and our high ground, the trials and chaotic circumstances of this world would have drowned us in despair and judgment.

Third, we would have remained trapped like a bird in a hunter's snare (Psalm 124:7). A snare is designed to catch the unsuspecting and hold them fast until the predator arrives. Apart from God’s intervention, we would have no power to break the trap of our own sin or the deceptions of the enemy.

Theologically, this passage underscores our total dependence on God's providence. It reminds us that we are not the masters of our own fate. If God were to withdraw His hand for even a moment, we would be lost. This truth is most clearly seen in the Gospel. Because of our sin, we were justly under the wrath of God and trapped by death. However, God was on our side through Jesus Christ. Jesus took the raging flood of God's judgment upon Himself at the cross so that we might be saved. He broke the snare of death through His bodily resurrection, providing us a way of escape that we could never have fashioned for ourselves.

As you reflect on your own journey, you can see that any season of safety or victory you have experienced is a gift from His hand. You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to be your Defender and Savior.

Consider your own life: Can you identify a moment where it is now clear that you only survived because the Lord intervened? Take time today to thank Him specifically for being the one who broke the snare for you.

question: What did Jesus mean when He said it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to get into heaven?
answer:
When Jesus spoke these words, He was using a vivid hyperbole to illustrate that it is humanly impossible for anyone to earn their way into the kingdom of God. By comparing a camel, the largest animal in Palestine, to the tiny opening of a sewing needle, He presented a picture of total physical impossibility to show that wealth, status, and human effort provide no leverage for entering heaven Matthew 19:24; Mark 10:25; Luke 18:25.

In the culture of that day, many believed that wealth was a sign of God's special favor and that if anyone was sure to be saved, it was the rich. When Jesus spoke this, His disciples were "greatly astonished" and asked, "Who then can be saved?" Matthew 19:25. Jesus’ point was not that being rich is inherently a sin, but that riches often create a false sense of self-sufficiency. A rich person is frequently tempted to trust in their resources rather than recognizing their spiritual bankruptcy before a holy God.

The heart of this teaching is found in Jesus’ next statement: "With man this is impossible, but with God all things are possible" Matthew 19:26. This underscores the necessity of regeneration—a work of the Holy Spirit that gives new life to a dead heart. Justification is not a ladder we climb by our own merit or success; it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone. 

The rich young man who had just walked away from Jesus was unwilling to part with his "great possessions" because they were his idol. To enter the kingdom, one must come like a child, empty-handed and fully dependent on the mercy of God. We all face the danger of holding onto something—be it money, reputation, or our own perceived "goodness"—more tightly than we hold onto Christ.

If you find yourself wondering how you can stand before a holy God, remember that the Gospel is the news of what Jesus has done because we could not do it ourselves. God is perfectly holy, and our sin separates us from Him. Yet, in His great love, Jesus Christ—who is truly God and truly man—lived the perfect life we failed to live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. 

You cannot squeeze through the needle’s eye by your own strength, but you can be saved through the power of God. I invite you to turn from your sins and trust in Jesus Christ alone as your Lord and Savior today for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it in your life that feels most difficult to surrender to Him?

As a practical step in your walk with the Lord, I encourage you to set aside time this week to pray through Psalm 139:23–24, asking the Holy Spirit to reveal any "riches" or self-dependencies you are trusting in more than Christ. Share what you discover with a trusted believer or an elder at your local church for prayer and accountability.

question: How do we decide which books belong in the Bible since the Bible does not say which books belong in the Bible?
answer:
The collection of sixty six books we call the Bible was not created by a human committee, but was recognized by the early church as the uniquely inspired Word of God. We call this collection the canon, which comes from a word meaning a rule or standard. While the Bible does not contain a printed index of its own books, it provides the marks of its own authority, which the people of God recognized through the guidance of the Holy Spirit.

The church did not grant authority to these books; rather, the books possessed authority from the moment they were written because they were breathed out by God (2 Timothy 3:16). The historical process of recognizing the canon involved several vital markers.

First, for the Old Testament, we look to the witness of Jesus Christ Himself. He affirmed the three divisions of the Hebrew Scriptures, the Law, the Prophets, and the Psalms (Luke 24:44). He treated the Old Testament as the final, authoritative word of God that cannot be broken (John 10:35). The books we have in our Old Testament today are the very same ones the Jewish community and Jesus recognized as Scripture.

Second, for the New Testament, the early church used three primary criteria to recognize God's Word. One was apostolicity, meaning a book was written by an apostle or a close associate of an apostle, such as Mark or Luke. The church is built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets (Ephesians 2:20). Another was orthodoxy, meaning the book's teaching was consistent with the known truth revealed in the rest of Scripture. The third was catholicity, or universal reception, meaning the book was recognized and used by the body of Christ across the known world from the beginning.

Ultimately, we believe in the canon because of the providence of God and the internal testimony of the Holy Spirit. Just as sheep recognize the voice of their shepherd, the people of God recognize the voice of their Lord in the pages of Scripture (John 10:27). The Holy Spirit works through the Word to convince our hearts of its divine origin and truth.

It is a great comfort to know that the same God who was powerful enough to inspire the Scriptures is also faithful enough to preserve them for His people. This Word is not a collection of religious opinions, but the very source of life. These Scriptures testify of Jesus Christ, the Son of God, who took on human nature, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins (John 5:39; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death.

I invite you to trust in this Jesus today, for the Bible tells us that everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved (Romans 10:13). As you study these sixty six books, I encourage you to begin your reading with a simple prayer, asking the Holy Spirit to open your eyes to behold wondrous things from His law (Psalm 119:18). Turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What is it that makes you hesitant to trust that God has preserved His Word for you?

question: What does it mean to walk by the Spirit (Galatians 5:16)?
answer:
To walk by the Spirit means to conduct your daily life in a state of constant dependence on the Holy Spirit, allowing His power and the truth of His Word to govern your desires and your decisions. It is the rhythmic practice of yielding your will to God's will so that the character of Christ is formed in you.

In the Greek text, the word for "walk" refers to one's manner of life or habitual conduct. This is the essence of progressive sanctification, the process by which a believer is set apart from sin and made more like Jesus. It is not a call to try harder in your own strength, but a call to rely more fully on the Spirit who dwells within you. As the Apostle Paul writes, "But I say, walk by the Spirit, and you will not gratify the desires of the flesh" (Galatians 5:16).

Walking by the Spirit involves two simultaneous movements. First, there is the mortification of sin, which is the active putting to death of the deeds of the body (Romans 8:13). Second, there is vivification, or being made alive to righteousness, as you feast on the Word of God and submit to His leading. There is a fierce conflict within every believer because "the flesh craves what is contrary to the Spirit, and the Spirit what is contrary to the flesh" (Galatians 5:17). You cannot win this battle by legalistic rules, but only by the superior power of the Holy Spirit.

This supernatural life is only possible for those who have been reconciled to God. Because God is holy and we are all sinful by nature and choice, we are naturally separated from Him. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. When you trust in Him, God gives you His Spirit as a seal and a guide. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

To practically begin walking by the Spirit today, I encourage you to start your morning by acknowledging your inability to live righteously on your own. Ask the Holy Spirit specifically to control your tongue and your heart in your very first interaction today, and meditate on a single "fruit of the Spirit," such as patience or kindness, from Galatians 5:22-23.

Does the struggle between your old desires and your new life in Christ ever feel overwhelming to you?

question: What does the Bible mean when it says "The fool says in his heart, 'There is no God'"?
answer:
The Bible defines the "fool" in Psalm 14:1 and Psalm 53:1 not as someone with a low intelligence or a lack of education, but as someone who is morally and spiritually defiant. This "folly" is a deep seated rejection of God’s authority, where a person chooses to live as if there is no divine Judge to whom they must give an account.

The phrase "in his heart" is significant because, in Scripture, the heart represents the center of a person’s will, affections, and reasoning. This suggests that atheism or the rejection of God is often a moral choice rather than a purely intellectual one. The Apostle Paul explains this further in Romans 1:18–20, stating that God has made His existence and attributes clearly known through "general revelation" (the created world), so that all people are without excuse. When a person says there is no God, they are suppressing the truth that God has already placed within their conscience and displayed in the stars above.

You may wonder how the Bible can call someone a fool when many who deny God are brilliant scientists or philosophers. From a biblical perspective, the greatest form of wisdom is "the fear of the LORD" (Proverbs 9:10). Therefore, to possess great earthly knowledge while missing the most fundamental reality of the universe (the Creator) is viewed as the height of spiritual tragedy. It is a refusal to see what is plainly visible because the heart prefers its own autonomy over God’s Lordship.

The Bible teaches that this condition is not unique to a few, but is a reflection of the fallen human nature we all share. We have all, at various times, lived as "practical atheists," acting as if God does not see or care about our choices. This rebellion is what the Bible calls sin, and it separates us from the holy and loving God who made us.

However, the Gospel provides a beautiful remedy for our spiritual blindness. While we were yet sinners and rebels, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus lived a perfect life of wisdom and obedience, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His victory, He offers forgiveness and a "new heart" to anyone who will stop hiding from the truth.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. As an actionable step, I encourage you to read the first three chapters of the book of Romans and ask God to reveal His truth to your heart as you read.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?

question: What is onanism? Is onanism a sin?
answer:
Onanism is a term derived from the historical account of Onan in the book of Genesis, referring specifically to the practice of withdrawing during sexual intercourse to prevent conception. In a broader theological and historical context, the term has also been used to describe masturbation or any sexual act that intentionally frustrates the procreative and unitive design of the marital act.

In the biblical account, Onan was commanded by his father to fulfill the requirements of a Levirate marriage by providing an heir for his deceased brother. Genesis 38:8-9. Onan, however, chose to waste his seed on the ground to avoid fathering a child that would not legally be considered his own. This act was not merely a private physical choice, but a public act of rebellion, greed, and a failure to love his neighbor as himself. The Scripture states plainly that what he did was wicked in the sight of the Lord, and God put him to death. Genesis 38:10.

Is onanism a sin? Yes, it is a sin because it violates the fundamental purposes for which God created human sexuality. Scripture teaches that our bodies are not our own but are temples of the Holy Spirit, to be used for God’s glory. 1 Corinthians 6:19-20. Whether defined as the specific act of Onan or as masturbation, it is a sin for several reasons:

First, it is a rejection of stewardship. God designed the sexual union within marriage to be both unitive (joining the husband and wife) and procreative (open to the blessing of children). Genesis 1:28; Genesis 2:24. When someone intentionally separates the pleasure of the act from its covenantal and reproductive purposes, they are attempting to take the gift of God while rejecting the Giver's design.

Second, in the form of masturbation, it almost always involves the sin of lust. Our Lord Jesus taught that anyone who looks at a woman with lustful intent has already committed adultery with her in his heart. Matthew 5:27-28. Sexual purity is not merely about external actions but about the holiness of the mind and heart.

We must speak the truth with love, for many struggle with these patterns of behavior and feel a heavy weight of shame. The holiness of God is a consuming fire that cannot tolerate sin, yet His mercy is a fountain that never runs dry for the repentant. We have all fallen short of God’s standard of purity and deserve His judgment. However, the Gospel brings hope to the sexually broken. Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human flesh, lived a life of perfect purity that we could never live, and died on the cross as a substitute for sinners. He bore the wrath we deserved so that we might be reconciled to God. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

If you find yourself entangled in sexual sin, do not despair or hide in the shadows. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone, not by our own efforts to "clean ourselves up" first.

As a practical step toward walking in purity, I encourage you to bring this struggle into the light by speaking with a faithful elder or a mature brother in Christ at your local church. Accountability and the regular means of grace, such as the preaching of the Word and prayer, are essential tools God uses for our sanctification. Turn your eyes away from selfish pleasure and fix them upon the beauty of Christ, who satisfies the soul far more than any passing shadow of this world. What is it that makes it difficult for you to trust God's design for your body today?
