However, the prophets also looked forward to a day when the Messiah would bring an end to all conflict, as seen in Isaiah 2:4, which says, "He will judge between the nations and will settle disputes for many peoples. They will beat their swords into plowshares and their spears into pruning hooks. Nation will not take up sword against nation, nor will they train for war anymore."

In the New Testament, the focus shifts toward the spiritual nature of the believer's struggle and the coming kingdom of Christ. Jesus, the Prince of Peace, taught His followers to love their enemies and to be peacemakers. Matthew 5:9; Matthew 5:44. He also warned that "wars and rumors of wars" would characterize the age before His return. Matthew 24:6. While Jesus did not explicitly forbid Christians from serving in the military, He emphasized that His kingdom is not of this world and is not spread through physical violence. John 18:36.

Historically, Christians have held two primary views regarding participation in war:

1. Just War Theory: Many believers, following the tradition of thinkers like Augustine and Thomas Aquinas, believe that the state has been given the "sword" by God to restrain evil and protect the innocent. Romans 13:4. This view holds that war can be a necessary evil if it meets specific criteria, such as being fought for a just cause, with right intent, and as a last resort.
2. Pacifism: Other faithful Christians believe that the commands of Jesus to turn the other cheek and love one's enemies prohibit believers from taking up arms under any circumstances. Matthew 5:39. They see the early church's frequent refusal to serve in the Roman legions as a model for modern discipleship.

Regardless of these secondary views, the Bible is clear that our primary battle is not against flesh and blood, but against spiritual forces of evil. Ephesians 6:12. We are called to put on the full armor of God to stand firm against the schemes of the devil. Ephesians 6:11.

Ultimately, the Bible points us to the final victory of Jesus Christ. He will return personally and bodily to judge the living and the dead, putting a definitive end to all rebellion and suffering. Revelation 19:11-16. Until that day, the greatest peace any person can find is not the absence of national conflict, but peace with God through the Lord Jesus Christ. Because of our sin, we were once enemies of God, but we are reconciled to Him through the sacrificial death and bodily resurrection of His Son. Romans 5:1; Romans 5:10.

If you find your heart troubled by the wars of this world, I encourage you to turn to Jesus Christ today. Repent of your sins and trust in Him alone for forgiveness and eternal life. You might begin by reading through the Gospel of John to see the love and authority of the Savior who overcame the world. John 16:33. An actionable step you can take today is to pray specifically for the leaders of nations, asking God to grant them wisdom and to bring peace so that the Gospel may be spread without hindrance. 1 Timothy 2:1-2.

question: Are there errors in the Qur'an (Koran)?
answer:
From the perspective of biblical truth and historical record, the Qur'an contains significant contradictions to the established Word of God, which Christians recognize as errors regarding the nature of God and the facts of history. You may wonder how two books claiming to be from the same God could offer such differing accounts, or perhaps you have heard the argument that the Bible was corrupted over time to create these discrepancies. However, we believe the Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God, preserved by His power for all generations (2 Timothy 3:16; Psalm 119:160).

The most profound contradiction concerns the historical fact of the crucifixion of Jesus Christ. The Qur'an claims that Jesus was not killed or crucified, but that it only appeared so to the onlookers (Surah 4:157). In contrast, the New Testament provides multiple eyewitness accounts of Christ's death on the cross, a fact also supported by non-Christian Roman and Jewish historians of the first century (Matthew 27:35, 50; John 19:30-34). To deny the crucifixion is to deny the very heart of the Gospel, for without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins (Hebrews 9:22).

Additionally, the Qur'an contains a misunderstanding of the Christian doctrine of the Trinity. It suggests that Christians worship three gods (Father, Mother Mary, and Son), and it explicitly denies that Jesus is the Son of God (Surah 5:116; Surah 9:30). The Bible teaches that there is only one God who eternally exists in three persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19; 2 Corinthians 13:14). Jesus is truly God and truly man, the eternal Word made flesh (John 1:1, 14).

These are not merely different "perspectives" but fundamental disagreements on historical events and the character of God. Because the Bible is our final authority, any text that contradicts the historical reality of the cross or the deity of Christ is seen as being in error.

This matters eternally because your standing before a holy God depends entirely on the work of Jesus Christ. The Bible teaches that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23). Because God is perfectly just, He must punish sin, but because He is perfectly loving, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or religious devotion (Ephesians 2:8-9). Jesus said, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

question: What is the spiritual significance to a déjà vu experience?
answer:
The Bible does not provide a specific teaching on the phenomenon of déjà vu, which suggests we should treat it as a subjective experience of the mind rather than a source of spiritual truth or revelation. While these moments can feel profound or mysterious, they are generally understood in a biological sense as a brief glitch in how the brain processes memory and current perception.

From a biblical perspective, however, we can view such experiences through the lens of God's Providence. Even though the feeling of having been somewhere before may be a trick of the mind, it serves as a reminder that our lives are not a series of random accidents. God is sovereign over all of time, declaring the end from the beginning and ordering every step of His children (Isaiah 46:10). If a moment of déjà vu causes you to pause, let it be an occasion to acknowledge that God is present in that very moment, just as He has been present in all your yesterdays and will be in all your tomorrows (Psalm 139:1-5).

You may wonder if these experiences suggest a hidden spiritual message or perhaps a previous life. While some secular or eastern worldviews propose reincarnation, the Word of God is clear that each person is created as a unique individual who lives once. Scripture states that man is appointed to die once, and after that to face judgment (Hebrews 9:27). This truth anchors our significance not in a cycle of past lives, but in the life we have been given now by our Creator.

The true spiritual significance we should seek is not found in the fleeting sensations of the mind, but in the objective reality of the Gospel. Our greatest mystery is not why we feel we have been somewhere before, but how a holy God could love and redeem sinful people. We have all turned away from God's perfect standard, falling short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Yet, in His mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is not achieved by our own efforts or by deciphering spiritual mysteries, but is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone. When we trust in Him, we are justified, declared righteous before God, and given the promise of eternal life. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to begin each morning by reading a portion of the Psalms. This will help ground your mind in the unchanging truth of God's Word so that when your feelings or experiences seem uncertain, you can stand firmly on the rock of Scripture (Psalm 119:105).

question: What are the four beasts in Daniel chapter 7?
answer:
In the seventh chapter of the book of Daniel, the prophet receives a vision of four great beasts arising out of the sea, which represent four successive earthly kingdoms or empires that dominate the biblical world. This vision serves to demonstrate the absolute sovereignty of God over the rise and fall of human nations and the ultimate victory of His eternal kingdom.

The first beast was like a lion with the wings of an eagle (Daniel 7:4). This symbolizes the Babylonian Empire, known for its majesty and swift military conquests. The plucking of its wings and it being made to stand on two feet like a man reflects the humbling and restoration of King Nebuchadnezzar, as recorded in Daniel 4:34-37.

The second beast resembled a bear, raised up on one of its sides with three ribs in its mouth (Daniel 7:5). This represents the Medo-Persian Empire. The fact that it was raised on one side signifies the dominance of the Persians over the Medes within that alliance, and the ribs likely represent the major territories it conquered to establish its power.

The third beast was like a leopard with four wings of a bird on its back and four heads (Daniel 7:6). This symbolizes the Greek Empire under Alexander the Great. The wings represent the incredible speed of Alexander's conquests, while the four heads represent the four administrative divisions of the empire that emerged among his generals after his death.

The fourth beast was different from the others, described as terrifying, frightening, and very powerful, with large iron teeth and ten horns (Daniel 7:7). This represents the Roman Empire, which was characterized by its crushing military force and long-standing legal and political influence. Within this vision, the fourth beast also points toward a future configuration of power and the appearance of a boastful "little horn" who opposes God's people.

While these earthly kingdoms are depicted as "beastly" and predatory, the vision shifts beautifully to the Ancient of Days and the Son of Man. We see that Jesus Christ, the Son of Man, is given an everlasting dominion that will not pass away (Daniel 7:13-14). This gives us great hope, for while earthly governments may be chaotic or even oppressive, our King sits upon the throne of the universe.

In your own life, you may feel overwhelmed by the "beasts" of this world, whether they be political unrest, personal trials, or spiritual opposition. Remember that the same God who revealed the end from the beginning holds your life in His hands. I encourage you to set aside time this week to read Daniel chapter 7 in its entirety, and as you do, pray for a heart that rests in God's sovereign providence rather than in the shifting powers of this world. What specific worry can you hand over to the King of kings today?

question: What did Paul mean when he said, “I die daily” in 1 Corinthians 15:31?
answer:
In 1 Corinthians 15:31, the Apostle Paul is primarily referring to the constant physical peril and the imminent threat of martyrdom he faced every day for the sake of the Gospel. He lived in such a way that his life was perpetually at risk because of his unwavering commitment to preaching the risen Christ.

This statement serves as a vital link in Paul's defense of the bodily resurrection of believers. He reasons that it would be foolish to face "danger every hour" (1 Corinthians 15:30) and to risk his life daily if there were no hope of a future resurrection. Paul’s boldness was not rooted in a death wish, but in the historical fact that Jesus Christ died for our sins, was buried, and was raised on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). Because Christ has conquered the grave, Paul could face the "beasts" of Ephesus and the threats of his enemies with a heart full of hope (1 Corinthians 15:32).

In a broader theological sense, this passage also touches upon the vital work of **progressive sanctification**. While Paul's immediate context was physical danger, the Christian life involves a daily surrender of the self. Jesus taught that anyone who wishes to follow Him must "deny himself and take up his cross daily" (Luke 9:23). This involves the mortification of sin, which is the intentional, Spirit empowered putting to death of the old self and its worldly desires (Romans 8:13; Colossians 3:5).

To "die daily" is to live with an eternal perspective that counts the comforts of this world as secondary to the glory of the next. It is the quiet confidence that because He lives, we shall live also. If you find yourself holding tightly to the things of this world or fearing the future, remember that Christ has removed the sting of death (1 Corinthians 15:55). 

As a practical step in your walk with the Lord, I encourage you to begin your morning by identifying one specific selfish ambition or anxious thought. Bring it before God in prayer and consciously yield it to His sovereign will, asking the Holy Spirit to give you the strength to live for His glory rather than your own.

If you have not yet trusted in the Lord Jesus, I invite you to turn from sin and place your faith in Him alone. He is the only one who provides forgiveness and the promise of a resurrection like His own (John 11:25). What is it that holds you back from trusting Him today?

question: What does the Bible say about motivation?
answer:
The Bible teaches that motivation is primarily a matter of the heart and that the highest purpose for all human action is the glory of God. While we often focus on outward results, the Scriptures remind us that "All a man’s ways seem right to him, but the LORD weighs the motives" (Proverbs 16:2).

In the Christian life, our primary driver is not the fear of punishment or the desire for earthly applause, but a deep-seated love for our Creator and Redeemer. We are called to live *coram Deo*, which means living before the face of God, under His authority, and for His honor. The Apostle Paul writes, "So whether you eat or drink or whatever you do, do it all to the glory of God" (1 Corinthians 10:31). This perspective shifts our daily work from a search for self-significance to a joyful act of service.

The wellspring of true, lasting motivation is the grace of God found in the Gospel. When we realize the magnitude of the love shown to us in Christ, our "duty" becomes a "delight." Paul explains this inward transformation, saying, "For Christ’s love compels us, because we are convinced that One died for all, therefore all died" (2 Corinthians 5:14). Because Jesus perfectly obeyed the Father and laid down His life for our sins, we are freed from the exhausting race of trying to earn God's favor. Instead, we work from a position of acceptance rather than for a position of acceptance. We are encouraged to "work at it with your whole heart, as working for the Lord and not for men" (Colossians 3:23).

However, we must honestly admit that our hearts are often divided, and our motives are frequently tainted by pride or selfishness. This constant struggle points us to our need for the Holy Spirit to renew our desires. True motivation is a fruit of sanctification, where God works in us "to will and to act in order to fulfill his good purpose" (Philippians 2:13).

**Practical Application:** Identify one task you find mundane or difficult this week. Before you begin, pause and consciously offer that work to God as an act of worship, asking Him to help you do it excellently for His sake rather than for your own credit.

If you find that your heart is restless or that you are striving for meaning in things that do not satisfy, remember that Jesus Christ came to reconcile you to the Father. He lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved so that through faith in Him, we might have new life. You can turn to God now, repent of seeking your own glory, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and a transformed heart. What holds you back from trusting Him with your whole heart today?

question: What does it mean to put off the old man (Ephesians 4:22)?
answer:
To put off the old man means to decisively renounce the sinful nature and the patterns of life that defined you before you were joined to Jesus Christ by faith. It is a spiritual stripping away of the corrupt identity, desires, and habits inherited from our first father, Adam, much like one would discard filthy, tattered garments that are no longer fit for use (Ephesians 4:22).

The old man is the person we once were, alienated from God and enslaved to deceitful lusts. When a person trusts in Christ, a fundamental change occurs: the old self is crucified with Him so that we might no longer be slaves to sin (Romans 6:6). However, the remnants of these old ways often cling to our daily experience. To put off this old nature is to actively refuse to let those former sins define our behavior or rule our hearts. This is not a call to mere self-improvement, but a command to mortify the sin that remains within us.

This process is part of progressive sanctification. It is never accomplished in your own strength, but through the power of the Holy Spirit who dwells in every believer. We are commanded to be renewed in the spirit of our minds and to put on the new man, which is created to be like God in true righteousness and holiness (Ephesians 4:23–24). You are laying aside the old life because God has already given you a new life and a new identity in His Son (Colossians 3:9–10).

If you find yourself discouraged by the persistence of old habits, look to the cross of Christ. The Gospel tells us that God is holy and our sin separated us from Him, but Jesus lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death to pay the debt we owed. He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

For a practical next step, identify one specific habit from your old life that you still struggle with, such as bitterness or dishonest speech. Spend time in prayer today, naming that sin specifically to God and asking Him to replace that desire with a hunger for His righteousness. Seek out a mature believer in your local church to pray with you and provide accountability, as the prayer of a righteous person has great power (James 5:16).

question: What is a freethinker?
answer:
A freethinker is generally defined as someone who forms their opinions and beliefs based on human reason and logic alone, intentionally independent of religious authority, tradition, or divine revelation. In a modern context, this term is frequently adopted by those who identify as atheists, agnostics, or secular humanists, as they prize the autonomy of the human mind as the ultimate arbiter of truth.

From a biblical perspective, we understand the desire for intellectual honesty and the rejection of empty tradition. However, the Scriptures teach that human reason is not a neutral or perfect tool. Because of the fall of man, our minds have been darkened by sin, a concept often referred to as the noetic effects of the fall. This means that while we can and should use our minds to investigate the world, our reasoning is prone to error when it attempts to function independently of God, who is the source of all truth (Colossians 2:8; Romans 1:21).

True freedom of thought is not found in autonomy from our Creator, but in being reconciled to Him so that we can see reality as it truly is. The Bible declares that "The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge" (Proverbs 1:7). Without acknowledging God, the freethinker essentially makes a god of their own intellect, which is a shifting foundation for life and eternity.

My friend, you may value the pursuit of truth above all else, and that is a noble desire. Yet, you might wonder how anyone can claim to have the absolute truth in a world of so many conflicting ideas. The Bible does not present itself as one philosophy among many, but as the historical record of God’s intervention in human history. It reveals that God is holy and supreme, while we are separated from Him by our sin and self-will. Because of His great love, God the Son became man in the person of Jesus Christ. He lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death.

Salvation and the restoration of our minds are found by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own intellectual achievements or good works. Jesus Himself said, "If you continue in My word, you are truly My disciples. Then you will know the truth, and the truth will set you free" (John 8:31-32).

I invite you to consider that the most profound use of your reason is to examine the claims of Jesus Christ. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus who died and rose for you. He is the only Mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5).

As an actionable next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. As you read, ask God to reveal the truth to your mind and heart. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

question: What is an overseer in the church?
answer:
An overseer is a man called by God and recognized by the local church to provide spiritual leadership, protection, and instruction to the congregation. The term comes from the Greek word *episkopos*, which carries the meaning of one who watches over or gazes upon a group to ensure their well-being and adherence to the truth.

In the New Testament, the office of overseer is functionally identical to the office of elder (*presbuteros*) and pastor or shepherd (*poimen*). We see these terms used interchangeably in passages such as Acts 20:17, 28, where Paul calls for the "elders" of the church and then charges them to "care for the church of God" as "overseers." Peter likewise exhorts "elders" to "shepherd the flock of God" by "exercising oversight" (1 Peter 5:1-2). This indicates that while the titles may highlight different aspects of the role, such as maturity (elder), protection (overseer), or feeding (pastor), they refer to the same group of qualified men leading a local body of believers.

The primary responsibilities of an overseer are to preach the Word, guard the church against false teachers, and model a life of godliness (Titus 1:9; Hebrews 13:7). Because this office carries great spiritual weight, the Bible sets forth strict moral and functional qualifications. An overseer must be above reproach, the husband of one wife, sober minded, self controlled, respectable, hospitable, able to teach, and one who manages his own household well (1 Timothy 3:1-7; Titus 1:5-9).

Within historic Christianity, there are differing views on how this office is structured. Many traditions, including Reformed Baptists and Presbyterians, believe the New Testament prescribes a plurality of elders or overseers within each local church who lead together. Other traditions, such as the Anglican, Lutheran, or Roman Catholic churches, maintain an "episcopal" form of government where an overseer (often called a Bishop) holds a distinct office of authority over multiple local congregations and their clergy. Both views seek to honor the biblical call for order and oversight in the house of God.

Ultimately, every human overseer serves under the authority of Jesus Christ, whom the Apostle Peter calls the Shepherd and Overseer of our souls (1 Peter 2:25). It is a beautiful thing when a church is led by men who love Christ and tenderly care for His people with the truth of the Gospel.

As a practical step of discipleship, I encourage you to read through the qualifications in 1 Timothy 3:1-7 this week. As you do, pray specifically for the leaders of your local church, asking God to grant them the wisdom, courage, and humility needed to watch over your soul and the souls of your brothers and sisters in Christ.

question: Someone gave me a prophetic word/prophesied over me. What is the interpretation?
answer:
It is understandable that you would feel a sense of weight or curiosity after someone has spoken a word over you. In moments like these, we must look to the light of God's Word, which is the only lamp for our feet and light for our path. Psalm 119:105.

The interpretation of any such word must begin with the absolute sufficiency of the Holy Scriptures. The Bible is the complete and final revelation of God's will for His people. 2 Timothy 3:16–17. Because God does not contradict Himself, any word spoken by a person must be measured against the clear teaching of the Bible. If what was said contradicts Scripture, you must reject it immediately. If it encourages you toward something the Bible already commands, such as holiness, service, or faith, then the encouragement comes from the truth already revealed in the Word.

Within the history of the church, many faithful believers hold that the miraculous gift of foretelling or receiving new revelation ceased once the New Testament was completed. Hebrews 1:1–2 tells us that while God spoke in various ways in the past through the prophets, in these last days He has spoken to us by His Son. Therefore, we do not look for new interpretations or hidden meanings outside of what has been once for all delivered to the saints. Jude 1:3. I cannot provide a personal interpretation of a word spoken over you, as only the Word of God serves as our infallible and inerrant guide.

If this word has caused you confusion or distress, your next step should be to take it to the elders of your local church. God has provided shepherds to help protect and guide the flock. Acts 20:28. Do not make major life decisions based on a subjective impression or a word from another person. Instead, pray for wisdom and seek the counsel of mature, godly leaders who know you and are committed to the Bible.

Ultimately, the greatest word ever spoken to humanity is the Gospel. We were all separated from a holy God by our sin, but God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ. He lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord of all. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What was the specific nature of the word given to you, and does it align with what you know of God's character and commands in the Bible?

question: What is/isn’t a Christian married couple allowed to do, sexually?
answer:
Biblical marriage is the sacred and exclusive domain for sexual intimacy, designed by God for the procreation of children, the unique knitting together of two souls into one flesh, and the mutual delight of the husband and wife. Within the covenant of marriage, God provides a wide field of Christian liberty, declaring that the marriage bed is undefiled (Hebrews 13:4).

The Bible does not provide a restrictive list of specific physical acts that are permitted or forbidden between a husband and wife. Instead, Scripture provides three primary boundaries that govern the intimacy of a Christian couple.

First, sexual intimacy must be exclusive. Any act that introduces a third party, whether physically through adultery or mentally through the use of pornography, is a violation of the marriage covenant and a sin against God and one's spouse (Exodus 20:14; Matthew 5:27–28). The intimacy of the marriage bed is a private sanctuary intended only for the two people who have made a lifelong vow to one another.

Second, sexual intimacy must be governed by mutual consent and sacrificial love. The Apostle Paul teaches that the husband and wife do not have exclusive authority over their own bodies but share that authority with their spouse (1 Corinthians 7:3–5). This is not a license for one spouse to demand or coerce the other; rather, it is a call to serve one another with tenderness. Any act that causes pain, degredation, or deep discomfort to a spouse is a failure to walk in the "agape" love that Christ has for His Church (Ephesians 5:25–28).

Third, sexual intimacy must be guided by the conscience of both individuals. If an act causes a spouse to feel they are violating their devotion to God or their sense of holiness, it should not be practiced. Scripture teaches that whatever does not proceed from faith is sin (Romans 14:23). A couple should never feel pressured by cultural trends to engage in activities that trouble their spirits or hinder their ability to pray together.

In summary, as long as an act is exclusive to the marriage, consensual, and does not violate the conscience of either spouse, the couple is free to enjoy one another as a gift from the Creator (Proverbs 5:18–19). This physical union is a beautiful, earthly shadow of the profound spiritual union between Christ and His people.

For a practical next step, I encourage you and your spouse to set aside a time to talk openly and gently about your preferences, boundaries, and any areas where you might better serve one another's joy. Ask God together in prayer to help you honor Him through your physical relationship, seeking to reflect His selfless love in your most private moments.

question: Why is God going to give us a white stone with a new name (Revelation 2:17)?
answer:
The white stone and the new name promised in Revelation 2:17 serve as a beautiful assurance of the believer's absolute acquittal and intimate union with Jesus Christ. In the ancient world, a white stone carried several deep meanings that help us understand the richness of God's grace toward those who remain faithful to Him.

In a legal context, a white stone was often used by a judge to signify a verdict of innocence, while a black stone meant condemnation. For the Christian, this signifies that in Christ, there is now no condemnation, as we have been justified by His blood (Romans 8:1). It is the Father's public declaration that you are accepted in the Beloved. Additionally, white stones were used as tokens of admission to special banquets or rewards for victors in athletic games. Receiving this stone from the Lord Jesus is your permanent invitation to the marriage supper of the Lamb (Revelation 19:9).

The new name written on the stone, known only to the one who receives it, speaks of a profound, personal intimacy. It signifies a transformation of character and a special relationship with the Savior that is unique to you. Just as God changed the names of Abram to Abraham or Simon to Peter to reflect their new calling and nature, this name marks your new identity as a child of God (Isaiah 62:2; John 1:12). It is a secret of love between the Redeemer and the redeemed, confirming that you are known, valued, and eternally His.

To receive this stone and the hidden manna of Christ's presence, one must be victorious. Scripture teaches that this victory belongs to those who trust in the Lord Jesus Christ, the Son of God (1 John 5:4-5). This victory is not earned by our own merit but is secured through Christ’s perfect life, His substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and His bodily resurrection on the third day.

If you find yourself weary or feeling anonymous in this world, remember that the Lord of Glory knows you personally and has prepared a place for you. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

As an actionable step in your walk with Him, I encourage you to set aside time this week to meditate on your identity in Christ using Ephesians 1. Ask the Lord in prayer to help you find your worth not in the labels of this world, but in the "new name" and the eternal acceptance He has promised to you. What is one area of your life where you struggle to believe you are fully accepted by God?

question: What is the meaning of the strange fire in Leviticus 10:1?
answer:
In the first verse of Leviticus 10, the "strange fire" (or unauthorized fire) refers to an offering of incense made by Nadab and Abihu, the sons of Aaron, that violated the specific instructions God had given for His worship. While the text does not explicitly detail every way their act was deficient, it emphasizes the core issue: they offered fire which the Lord "had not commanded them" (Leviticus 10:1). This event serves as a sobering demonstration of God’s holiness and the necessity of approaching Him with deep reverence and exact obedience.

Theologically, this passage underscores the character of God as holy and set apart. When fire came out from the presence of the Lord and consumed the two priests, God explained the judgment by saying, "By those who come near Me I will be treated as holy; and in the sight of all the people I will be honored" (Leviticus 10:3). This highlights what many call the regulative principle of worship, which teaches that God is to be worshipped only in the ways He has specifically authorized in His Word. To bring "strange fire" is to substitute human innovation, preference, or convenience for the divine commands of the Almighty (Exodus 30:9; Leviticus 16:12).

Practically, this account warns us against a casual or flippant attitude toward the things of God. It is easy to fall into the trap of thinking our sincerity or our "good intentions" are enough, but God desires our obedience and our honor for His specific truth. As we consider our own lives, we must ask if we are trying to approach God on our own terms or through the means He has provided.

This history points us ultimately to our need for a perfect Mediator. We are all like Nadab and Abihu in that we have naturally rebellious hearts that desire to do things our own way. Because God is a "consuming fire" (Hebrews 12:29), our unauthorized efforts to please Him by our own works would only lead to our destruction. However, the Gospel tells us that Jesus Christ came as the perfect High Priest who never offered strange fire. He lived a life of perfect obedience and then offered Himself as the final sacrifice on the cross. He took the fire of God’s righteous judgment against sin upon Himself so that we could be forgiven. 

Christ was buried and rose again on the third day, demonstrating His victory over death and providing the only way for us to draw near to a holy God (John 14:6). You can turn to God today, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive the gift of eternal life.

As an actionable step in your walk with the Lord, I encourage you to spend time this week reading through the book of Hebrews, specifically chapters 4 through 10. This will help you see how Christ fulfills and surpasses the Old Testament priesthood. When you pray, ask the Holy Spirit to cultivate in you a heart that trembles at His Word and rests fully in the finished work of Jesus.

question: Is it possible to sell your soul to the devil?
answer:
It is not possible to literally sell your soul to the devil because your soul is not your own property to trade. According to the Scriptures, God is the Creator and the ultimate owner of every human soul, as He declares in Ezekiel 18:4, "Behold, all souls are Mine."

The idea of a "Faustian bargain" or a binding legal contract with Satan is a product of folklore and literature rather than biblical truth. While the devil is described as the "god of this world" who blinds the minds of unbelievers in 2 Corinthians 4:4, he does not possess the authority to purchase what God has created. However, the Bible does teach a more sobering reality: apart from Christ, every human being is already in a state of spiritual bondage. Jesus taught that "everyone who sins is a slave to sin" in John 8:34. We do not need to make a deal to be lost, for we are born separated from God by our own nature and choices (Romans 3:23).

The wonderful news of the Gospel is that while we cannot sell our souls, we have been bought with a price far greater than any earthly treasure. Redemption is the theological term for Christ purchasing His people out of the marketplace of sin. This was not paid to the devil, but was a satisfaction of God’s own justice. You were redeemed "not with perishable things like silver or gold... but with the precious blood of Christ, a lamb without blemish or spot" (1 Peter 1:18-19).

If you feel as though you have "given yourself over" to evil or that you are beyond hope, please know that the sovereignty of God is greater than any past mistake or supposed "deal." God is holy and just, and our sin separates us from Him. Yet, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, to die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and to rise bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works or merit of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9).

No matter what darkness you feel you have walked into, the light of Christ is sufficient to lead you out. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What is it that makes you wonder if a soul could be lost in such a way? As an actionable step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible to see the heart of Jesus for those who feel lost or burdened. He promises in John 6:37, "Everyone the Father gives Me will come to Me, and the one who comes to Me I will never drive away."

question: What does the Bible say about obedience?
answer:
The Bible teaches that obedience is the sincere expression of our love for God and our recognition of His sovereign Lordship over our lives. It is the outward demonstration of a heart that has been transformed by His grace, serving as the fruit of our faith rather than the root of our salvation.

In the Old Testament, the prophet Samuel famously declared that "to obey is better than sacrifice" in 1 Samuel 15:22. This reveals that God is not looking for empty religious rituals, but for a heart that is yielded to His will. Throughout the Scriptures, obedience is consistently linked to our relationship with God. Jesus Himself made this the ultimate test of discipleship when He said, "If you love Me, keep My commandments" in John 14:15.

True biblical obedience is characterized by several key truths:

First, it is a response to God's love. We do not obey to get God to love us, but because He first loved us and gave His Son for us. Our standing before God is based on the perfect, active obedience of Jesus Christ, who lived the life we could not live and died the death we deserved. This is the doctrine of justification, where His righteousness is credited to us through faith alone. Because we are now children of God, our obedience becomes a joyful "thank you" to our Father.

Second, it is empowered by the Holy Spirit. In our own strength, we are unable to keep God's law perfectly. However, for those who are in Christ, the Holy Spirit works within us to produce the desire and the power to walk in His ways. This process is known as progressive sanctification. As Paul writes in Romans 6:17, believers have "obeyed from the heart that form of doctrine" to which they were delivered.

Third, it is a matter of the heart, not just external behavior. The Bible warns against "teaching as doctrines the commandments of men" or appearing righteous on the outside while the heart remains far from God. Real obedience involves the whole person, as James 1:22 exhorts us to "be doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving yourselves."

If you find yourself struggling to obey, look first to the cross of Christ. It is there that we see both the seriousness of our sin and the immensity of God's grace. When we behold His love, our hearts are stirred to follow Him more closely.

As a practical next step in your walk with the Lord, I encourage you to choose one specific command from the Sermon on the Mount, such as found in Matthew 5, 6, or 7, and ask the Holy Spirit in prayer to help you apply it to your life this week. Whether it is practicing secret generosity or forgiving someone who has wronged you, let your actions be a testimony of your trust in Him. For those who do not yet know Christ, remember that the first act of obedience God calls you to is to repent and believe the Gospel, trusting in Jesus Christ alone for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

question: What does it mean that God is our refuge and strength and an ever-present help in trouble (Psalm 46:1)?
answer:
To say that God is our refuge and strength is to affirm that He is the ultimate hiding place for the soul and the tireless source of power for the weary. This glorious truth, found in Psalm 46:1, declares that God is not a distant observer of our pain, but a Sovereign Protector who is immediately accessible to His children in their deepest moments of distress.

When the Bible calls God a refuge, it uses the imagery of a high tower or a fortified stronghold that cannot be shaken by the storms of life or the attacks of the enemy. As a refuge, He provides safety from the righteous judgment our sins deserve and shelter from the trials of a broken world (Psalm 91:1; Psalm 91:2). This protection is not found in our own efforts, but in the character and promises of God Himself.

As our strength, God provides the internal empowerment we lack. Our human resolve is finite and fragile, but the strength God gives is the same omnipotence that sustains the universe (Isaiah 40:28; Isaiah 40:29; Isaiah 40:31). Because He is immutable and faithful, His power remains constant even when our heart and flesh fail (Psalm 73:26).

The phrase "ever-present help" carries the meaning of a help that has been tried, tested, and found sufficient. He is "very present," meaning He is closer to us than our own breath. He does not need to be summoned from a distance, for His Spirit dwells within those who belong to Him (Psalm 139:7; Psalm 139:8; Psalm 139:9; Psalm 139:10).

The greatest trouble any human face is the weight of sin and the reality of God's holy wrath. Yet, God in His mercy provided the ultimate refuge in His Son, Jesus Christ. Though we have all turned away from God and deserve His judgment, Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day (Romans 5:8; 1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4). He is the only Mediator between God and man, and through Him alone we find peace (1 Timothy 2:5; John 14:6).

If you find yourself overwhelmed by the "troubles" of life or the guilt of your own heart, know that this refuge is open to all who come to Him. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

As a practical step of discipleship, I encourage you to set aside time today to read the entirety of Psalm 46. As you read, speak these truths back to God in prayer, specifically naming the "troubles" you are facing and consciously entrusting them to His sovereign care. What specific fear is most heavy on your heart today that you need to bring to His refuge?

question: Should a Christian woman wear a bikini?
answer:
The question of what a Christian woman should wear, including a bikini, is a matter that touches on the biblical principles of modesty, stewardship of the body, and love for one's neighbor. While the Bible does not provide a specific dress code or mention modern swimwear, it offers timeless guidance for the heart that informs every outward choice.

The Scriptures teach that a woman's primary adornment should not be outward or merely physical, but should flow from a heart that is devoted to God. The Apostle Paul writes that women should adorn themselves in respectable apparel, with modesty and self-control, which is proper for women who profess godliness 1 Timothy 2:9–10. This concept of modesty is not about hiding the body because it is shameful, but about honoring the body as a temple of the Holy Spirit and ensuring that our appearance does not distract others from the glory of Christ 1 Corinthians 6:19–20.

Within historic Christianity, the application of these principles is often considered a tertiary matter, meaning it is an area where faithful believers may reach different conclusions based on their conscience and cultural context. 

Some Christians hold a more restrictive view, believing that the bikini is inherently designed to draw attention to the physical form in a way that can incite lust or promote vanity. They would argue that a Christian woman should choose swimwear that provides more coverage to protect her own heart from pride and to be a sisterly helper to her brothers in Christ by not presenting a stumbling block Matthew 5:28; Romans 14:13.

Other Christians believe that modesty is more about the motive of the heart and the specific context. They may feel that in certain settings, such as with family or in a culture where such attire is standard and not intended to be provocative, a bikini can be worn with a clear conscience. They emphasize Christian liberty and the idea that no specific garment is inherently sinful, provided the wearer is acting in faith and love Romans 14:22–23.

As you navigate this, remember that your identity is found in being a beloved daughter of the King, redeemed by the precious blood of Jesus Christ. He has called you out of darkness and into His marvelous light to reflect His character to the world 1 Peter 2:9. True beauty is found in the hidden person of the heart, with the imperishable beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit, which in God's sight is very precious 1 Peter 3:3–4.

A helpful way to examine your own heart is to ask: Does this choice reflect the self-control and holiness that the Holy Spirit is producing in me through sanctification? Am I seeking to honor God and serve my neighbors with my choices? 

As a practical next step, I encourage you to spend time in prayer, asking the Lord to give you wisdom and a sensitive conscience. It may also be very helpful to speak with a mature, godly woman in your local church whom you respect. Her perspective and shared experience can offer personal guidance that honors the Lord and respects your local community. Seek to do all things, including how you dress, for the glory of God 1 Corinthians 10:31.

question: Is Christian rap music appropriate?
answer:
The appropriateness of Christian rap music is a matter of Christian liberty and conscience, as the Bible does not command or forbid specific musical genres. Whether a song is appropriate depends primarily on its lyrical faithfulness to Scripture, the intent of the artist, and the effect it has on the listener's spiritual edification.

In the kingdom of God, music is a gift intended for the praise of the Creator and the encouragement of His people. The Apostle Paul instructs believers in Colossians 3:16 to let the word of Christ dwell in them richly as they teach and admonish one another with all wisdom through psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs, singing to God with gratitude in their hearts. If a rap song is saturated with the Truth of the Gospel and points the heart toward Christ, it can serve as a powerful tool for sanctification and evangelism.

However, faithful Christians hold different views on this tertiary issue, often falling into two main perspectives:

1. Many believers see rap as a neutral medium that can be redeemed for God's glory. They argue that because God is the author of rhythm and speech, these elements can be used to reach subcultures and generations that might not engage with traditional hymns. From this view, as long as the content is true, honorable, and pure, the style is a matter of cultural expression (1 Corinthians 10:31).

2. Other believers exercise caution, concerned that the historical associations of rap music (such as pride, rebellion, or worldly sensuality) may be difficult to separate from the message. They may prefer musical forms that they believe more naturally facilitate "reverence and awe" in worship (Hebrews 12:28). These brothers and sisters often emphasize the need for a clear distinction between the sacred and the secular.

Because this is a matter of Christian liberty, we must follow the wisdom of Romans 14:5, which says that each person should be fully convinced in his own mind. We must not judge those whose consciences allow them to enjoy such music, nor should those who listen to it look down on those who do not.

When discerning whether a particular song is appropriate for your ears, I encourage you to apply the "Philippians 4:8 filter." Ask yourself: Is this song true? Is it honorable? Is it right? Is it pure? Is it lovely? Is it admirable? If the lyrics promote the pride of life or the lust of the flesh, they are not fitting for a saint, regardless of the beat. But if the song humbles the sinner, exalts the Savior, and aligns with the infallible Word of God, it can be a source of great joy.

As a practical step, take a moment this week to examine the lyrics of the music you enjoy. Compare the message of the songs to the clear teaching of Scripture. If you find that certain music hinders your fellowship with God or stirs up worldly desires, have the courage to set it aside for the sake of your soul’s health.

Remember that while music can stir the emotions, it is the Gospel alone that has the power to save. If you find yourself searching for meaning or peace that music cannot provide, look to Jesus Christ. He is the eternal Word who took on flesh, lived a perfect life, died for our sins, and rose again so that we might have life. You can turn to Him now, repent of your sins, and trust in Him alone for forgiveness. What prevents you from resting fully in His grace today?

question: What is a "dark night of the soul"?
answer:
A "dark night of the soul" is a period of spiritual desolation, felt absence of God, or intense trial where a believer's usual comforts in the faith seem to vanish. It is a painful but purposeful season used by God to deepen a person's faith, stripping away a reliance on feelings and replacing it with a firm trust in His character and promises.

While the term originated with the sixteenth-century poet St. John of the Cross, the experience itself is deeply biblical. It is often a vital part of progressive sanctification, which is the process by which the Holy Spirit makes us more like Christ. In His divine providence, God may withdraw the conscious "sensible" sweetness of His presence to teach the soul to walk by faith and not by sight (2 Corinthians 5:7). This trial serves to purge our hearts of subtle idols and to prove that our love for God is based on who He is, rather than just the blessings He provides.

Scripture is filled with saints who walked through this thick darkness. Job cried out because he could not perceive God's presence in Job 23:8-9. David asked, "How long, O LORD? Will You forget me forever? How long will You hide Your face from me?" in Psalm 13:1. Even our Savior experienced the ultimate dark night upon the cross when He took the weight of our sin and cried, "My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me?" in Matthew 27:46. Because Christ was truly forsaken in our place, we can be certain that God will never truly leave nor forsake those who belong to Him (Hebrews 13:5).

In these moments, it is essential to remember that our standing before God does not depend on the warmth of our emotions, but on the objective work of Jesus Christ. He lived a life of perfect obedience, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave to secure our eternal life. Our hope is anchored in His finished work, which remains true even when our hearts feel cold or heavy.

If you are currently walking through such a darkness, do not interpret the silence of God as the absence of God. One actionable step you can take is to pray through the "Psalms of Lament," such as Psalm 13, Psalm 22, or Psalm 88. These passages provide a divinely inspired language for your pain, allowing you to be honest with God while you wait for Him to restore the joy of your salvation (Psalm 51:12).

If you have never placed your trust in Jesus Christ, I invite you to turn from your sin and look to Him today. He is the light that shines in the darkness, and He offers forgiveness and peace to all who come to Him in faith. What is it that might be holding you back from trusting Him today?

question: What does the Bible say about self-defense?
answer:
The Bible teaches that human life is sacred because every person is created in the image of God, a doctrine known as the Imago Dei (Genesis 1:27). Because life is a gift from the Creator, the Scriptures generally affirm the right and responsibility to protect that life from unjust harm, while simultaneously calling believers to a heart of peace and forgiveness.

In the Law of Moses, God provided a specific distinction regarding the defense of one’s home. In Exodus 22:2-3, it is stated that if a thief is caught breaking in at night and is struck and killed, the defender is not guilty of bloodshed. This suggests that when a threat is immediate and the intent is unknown, protecting one's household is permissible. However, the passage also warns against unnecessary killing if the immediate danger has passed, emphasizing that the goal is protection, not vengeance.

While the Lord Jesus taught His followers to "turn the other cheek" when faced with personal insults or legal slaps (Matthew 5:38-39), this was a call to renounce personal retaliation and pride, not a command to allow the slaughter of the innocent. In fact, shortly before His arrest, Jesus told His disciples, "If you have a cloak, sell it and buy a sword" (Luke 22:36). While this sword was not for spreading the Gospel by force, it served as a means of protection for travelers in a dangerous and fallen world.

The biblical framework for self-defense is often rooted in the duty to protect others. Scripture commands us to "Rescue the weak and needy; deliver them from the hand of the wicked" (Psalm 82:4) and to "Deliver those being led away to death" (Proverbs 24:11). Loving our neighbor as ourselves (Mark 12:31) includes the physical protection of our families and the vulnerable.

However, we must distinguish between the defensive use of force and a heart of malice. The Apostle Paul instructs believers, "If it is possible, as far as it depends on you, live at peace with everyone" (Romans 12:18). We are never to seek out conflict or rely on violence as a primary means of resolution, for "the wrath of man does not produce the righteousness of God" (James 1:20).

As you consider this, I encourage you to pray for a heart that values peace above all else while remaining a faithful steward of the lives God has placed in your care. A practical step would be to study the laws of your local community to ensure you act with wisdom and within the "governing authorities" that God has established for our protection (Romans 13:1). Above all, trust in the Lord as your ultimate shield and buckler (Psalm 91:4).

If you are wrestling with fear or the weight of living in a violent world, remember that true security is found in Jesus Christ alone. He faced the ultimate violence of the cross, not to defend Himself, but to save us from our sins. You can turn to Him today, repenting of sin and trusting in His death and resurrection for the peace that surpasses all understanding.

question: What was the significance of Jesus being dead for three days?
answer:
The three day period between the death of Jesus and His resurrection serves as the historical and theological proof of His victory over the grave and the fulfillment of God’s sovereign plan for redemption. By remaining in the tomb for this duration, the Lord Jesus demonstrated that His physical death was a reality and that His subsequent resurrection was a miraculous act of God rather than a mere recovery from injury.

The significance of these three days is multifaceted, rooted deeply in the testimony of the Scriptures.

First, this timeframe fulfills the prophecy of the Sign of Jonah. Jesus Himself declared that just as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so the Son of Man would be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth (Matthew 12:40). This duration serves as a divine seal, proving that Jesus is the promised Messiah who operates according to the precise timetable of the Father.

Second, the three days established the certainty of His death. In the ancient world, three days was often seen as the timeframe necessary to confirm that a person was truly deceased and that the soul had departed. By rising on the third day, Jesus left no room for the false claim that He had simply fainted or swooned. His heart had stopped, His breath had ceased, and He was buried in a sealed tomb (Matthew 27:59-66).

Third, the resurrection on the third day is the ultimate vindication of Christ’s work on the cross. While the crucifixion was the place where the penalty for our sin was paid through penal substitutionary atonement, the resurrection is the receipt that shows the payment was accepted by God the Father. As the Scripture says, He was delivered over to death for our sins and was raised to life for our justification (Romans 4:25).

This historical event is the very heart of the Gospel. The Bible teaches that God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our rebellion, we deserve eternal separation from Him. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die in the place of sinners. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, defeating sin and death forever (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that might be holding you back from resting your soul in His finished work?

For your own encouragement and study, I suggest you read through 1 Corinthians 15 this week. It provides a glorious explanation of why the resurrection is the cornerstone of our faith. As you read, consider the power of a Savior who has conquered the grave on your behalf.

question: Is the idea of chi compatible with the Christian faith?
answer:
The concept of chi, often defined as an impersonal, universal life force or energy that flows through all things, is fundamentally incompatible with the Christian faith. While the desire for health and harmony is understandable, the framework of chi originates from a worldview that stands in direct contrast to the biblical revelation of a personal, sovereign Creator who is distinct from His creation.

In Scripture, life is not a neutral energy to be balanced or manipulated, but a gift directly sustained by God. This is the doctrine of divine providence. The Bible teaches that God created the world out of nothing by the power of His Word, and He continues to uphold the universe by that same power. In Genesis 2:7, we see that man became a living being because God breathed the breath of life into him, not because an impersonal energy was channeled into him.

There are three primary areas where chi and Christianity diverge:

First, the nature of God. The concept of chi is rooted in monism or pantheism, the belief that everything is part of one divine substance or energy. Christianity teaches that God is a personal Being, existing eternally as Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. He is the Creator, and we are His creatures. There is a clear boundary between the Maker and the made, as seen in Colossians 1:16-17, which says that by Christ all things were created and in Him all things hold together.

Second, the source of life and power. Those who practice chi-based disciplines often seek to harness this energy for healing or spiritual enlightenment. However, the Bible teaches that our help comes from the Lord. Any spiritual power or "energy" that does not acknowledge the Lordship of Jesus Christ and the authority of His Word is a dangerous substitute. Acts 17:25-28 reminds us that God Himself gives to all mankind life and breath and everything, and that it is in Him that we live and move and have our being.

Third, the problem of the human condition. The chi worldview suggests that our primary problem is an "imbalance" of energy. The Bible identifies our true problem as sin, a moral rebellion against a holy God. No amount of energy balancing can solve the problem of guilt or the reality of death. Only the substitutionary atonement of Jesus Christ can reconcile us to God.

You may wonder if chi is simply a way to describe the natural biological processes or the "breath of life" mentioned in the Bible. However, while we recognize that God has designed the body with amazing complexity, we must not confuse physical biology with a spiritualized "energy" that claims to govern our destiny or connect us to the divine.

If you are searching for peace, healing, or a deeper connection to the source of life, I encourage you to look to the Person of Jesus Christ rather than an impersonal force. He is the Word made flesh who died for our sins and rose bodily from the grave to give us true and eternal life. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and a restored relationship with your Creator.

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John, which beautifully describes Jesus as the true Light and the source of all life. Consider how His personal love for you is far greater than any concept of impersonal energy. What makes the idea of chi appealing to you in your current journey?

question: What is the peace that passes all understanding?
answer:
The peace that surpasses all understanding is the supernatural gift of divine tranquility that guards the heart and mind of a believer, remaining steadfast even when earthly circumstances are in turmoil. This peace is not merely the absence of conflict or a psychological state of mind, but is a fruit of the Holy Spirit that defies human logic and earthly explanation.

The Apostle Paul describes this reality in his letter to the Philippians while he was himself in a Roman prison. He writes, "And the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard your hearts and your minds in Christ Jesus" (Philippians 4:7). The word used for "guard" carries the imagery of a Roman garrison standing watch over a city. Just as a soldier protects a gate, God's peace stands sentry over the inner life of the Christian, preventing anxiety from overrunning the soul.

To understand this peace, we must distinguish between "peace with God" and the "peace of God."

1. **Peace with God:** This is the foundation. Because of our sin, we were once enemies of God, separated from His holiness and subject to His righteous judgment. However, through the work of Jesus Christ, we are justified by faith. As it says in Romans 5:1, "Therefore, since we have been justified through faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ." This is a legal, objective standing where our warfare with the Creator has ended because Christ paid our debt on the cross.

2. **The Peace of God:** This is the subjective experience of that relationship. It is the quiet confidence that God is sovereign, that He is good, and that He is for us in Christ. It is a peace that can sing in the midst of a storm because it rests on the character of an unchanging God rather than the shifting sands of our situation.

The world offers a fragile peace based on favorable conditions, but Jesus says, "Peace I leave with you; My peace I give to you. I do not give to you as the world gives" (John 14:27). This peace is the result of a life of prayer and gratitude. Paul instructs us to not be anxious about anything, but in everything, by prayer and petition, with thanksgiving, to present our requests to God (Philippians 4:6). When we hand our burdens to the One who is supreme over all things, His peace settles upon us.

If you find your heart troubled today, remember that true peace is only found in a reconciled relationship with the Father. God is holy, and our sin has created a chasm we cannot bridge by our own efforts. Yet, God in His mercy sent Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the death we deserved. By His bodily resurrection, He defeated death and offers eternal life to all who believe.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Once you have peace with God through the cross, the peace of God will begin to rule in your heart.

As a practical step in your discipleship, I encourage you to identify one specific anxiety that is weighing on you today. Bring it to the Lord in prayer, naming it clearly, and then purposefully list three specific ways God has been faithful to you in the past. This practice of "prayer with thanksgiving" is the biblical doorway to the peace that surpasses understanding.

What holds you back from trusting Him with that burden today?

question: Is an eclipse a sign of the end times?
answer:
While the Bible explicitly mentions the sun being darkened as a sign associated with the return of Jesus Christ and the Day of the Lord, a regular solar eclipse is generally understood as a predictable part of God's created order rather than a specific supernatural omen of the immediate end. In His divine providence, God established the lights in the expanse of the heavens to serve as signs and for seasons, and for days and years, as recorded in Genesis 1:14.

Scripture describes profound celestial events that will precede the second coming of our Lord. The prophet Joel declared that the sun will be turned to darkness and the moon to blood before the coming of the great and awesome Day of the LORD, in Joel 2:31. Jesus echoed this in His Olivet Discourse, stating that immediately after the tribulation of those days, the sun will be darkened, and the moon will not give its light, in Matthew 24:29. These passages point to a unique, global, and likely supernatural darkness that transcends the brief and localized nature of a natural solar eclipse.

For the believer, any reminder of the vastness of the heavens should stir a sense of awe for the Creator and a sober reflection on our readiness for His return. You may wonder if these natural events are intended to stir our hearts to repentance or if they are mere coincidences of orbital mechanics. It is wise to remember that while we can predict the timing of an eclipse, Jesus taught that no one knows the day or the hour of His return, in Matthew 24:36. Therefore, our focus should not be on calculating dates through astronomical events, but on the state of our souls before a holy God.

The most important question is not when the sun will go dark, but whether you have been reconciled to the One who created the sun. We have all sinned and fallen short of the glory of God, earning a spiritual darkness and separation from Him. Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was bodily raised to life on the third day, proving His victory over death. 

He is coming again to judge the living and the dead and to make all things new. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from placing your full trust in Him today?

As a practical next step in your walk with God, I encourage you to read through the Gospel of Luke chapter 21 this week. As you read, consider the exhortation of Jesus to stay alert and pray for the strength to stand before the Son of Man. Let the wonder of the heavens lead you to a deeper prayer life, asking God to keep your heart focused on the eternal hope we have in Christ.

question: Who are the multitude of all nations, tribes, peoples, and tongues in Revelation 7:9?
answer:
The multitude described in Revelation 7:9 represents the complete, redeemed people of God gathered from every corner of the earth throughout all of human history. These are the individuals who have been rescued from the guilt and power of sin through the finished work of Jesus Christ, the Lamb of God.

This vast assembly is the direct fulfillment of the promise God made to Abraham that through his offspring all the nations of the earth would be blessed, as recorded in Genesis 12:3 and Genesis 22:18. While the preceding verses in Revelation describe a specific numbering of the tribes of Israel, this great multitude is one that "no one could number," signifying the overflowing abundance of God's mercy and the success of Christ's redemptive mission, which is further described in Revelation 5:9.

The passage provides beautiful details about their identity and current state. They are "clothed in white robes," which symbolizes the gift of perfect righteousness granted to them through justification by faith. They do not stand there based on their own merit, but because they have "washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb," as stated in Revelation 7:14. This points to the central doctrine of the atonement, where Christ’s sacrificial death serves as the only means by which a person can be reconciled to a holy God.

Regarding the "great tribulation" mentioned in Revelation 7:14, faithful Christians hold different views. Some believe this refers to a specific, intense period of suffering at the very end of the age. Others understand it as the general trials and persecutions that the church endures throughout the entire period between Christ’s first and second coming, as Jesus spoke of in John 16:33. Regardless of the specific timing of the tribulation, the primary truth is that God sovereignly preserves His people through every trial and brings them safely to His eternal presence.

If you wonder whether there is room for you in such a multitude, please know that the Gospel is a free invitation to all. Though we have all sinned and fallen short of the glory of God, as noted in Romans 3:23, God in His love sent His Son to be the propitiation for our sins. Jesus Christ lived a perfect life, died on the cross in the place of sinners, and rose bodily on the third day. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

As a practical step in your walk with God, consider how this vision of a diverse, global church might shape your own prayers. You might choose to pray specifically this week for the spread of the Gospel among a people group or nation different from your own, asking that God would continue to call His people from every tongue and tribe. Such a perspective helps us to look past our local concerns and rejoice in the grand, sovereign work God is doing across the whole world.

question: What is the counsel of the ungodly, and how do we not walk in it (Psalm 1:1)?
answer:
The counsel of the ungodly refers to the advice, worldviews, and moral philosophies of those who live and think independently of God's truth. It is the "wisdom of this age" that prioritizes human intuition, cultural trends, or personal desire over the sovereign Word of the Lord.

To walk in this counsel means to adopt these secular patterns as your own, allowing them to shape your decisions, values, and character. This often begins with a subtle listening to voices that do not fear God, which leads to "standing" in the way of sinners and finally "sitting" in the seat of mockers, as Psalm 1:1 describes. This progression shows how worldliness can slowly harden a heart against the Gospel. The Bible warns that there is a way that seems right to a man, but its end is the way of death in Proverbs 14:12.

We avoid walking in this counsel through the process of sanctification, which is the Holy Spirit's work of renewing our minds and conforming us to the image of Christ. We must be vigilant, as the Apostle Paul warns us to see to it that no one takes us captive through philosophy and empty deception, which are based on human tradition and the spiritual forces of the world rather than on Christ in Colossians 2:8.

To practically guard your life from ungodly counsel, you must intentionally replace the world's voice with God's voice. This is why the very next verse in the Psalm tells us that the blessed man's delight is in the law of the Lord, and on His law he meditates day and night in Psalm 1:2.

I encourage you to set a specific rhythm this week to dwell on the Scriptures before you listen to any other media or opinions. Ask the Lord in prayer to give you the gift of discernment, so that you may weigh every piece of advice against the perfect standard of His Word. Seek also the fellowship of a healthy local church where you can receive the wise, biblical counsel of elders and mature believers who will help you walk in the light.

question: Does heaven exist?
answer:
Yes, heaven exists as a literal and glorious reality. The Bible describes heaven not as a state of mind or a poetic metaphor, but as the actual dwelling place of God and the eternal home prepared for those who belong to Him.

Scripture presents heaven as a place of perfect holiness, joy, and the immediate presence of the Lord. Jesus spoke of it with absolute certainty, telling His disciples, "In My Father’s house are many rooms. If it were not so, would I have told you that I am going there to prepare a place for you?" (John 14:2). It is a realm where the curse of sin is finally and fully removed, and where God "will wipe away every tear from their eyes," and "there will be no more death or mourning or crying or pain" (Revelation 21:4).

While heaven is a place of rest, it is also a place of vibrant life and worship. Christians look forward to the "glorification" of their bodies and the renewal of all things, where we will see the face of our Savior and serve Him in a kingdom that will never end (Revelation 22:3-5).

The reality of heaven is inseparable from the person of Jesus Christ. Because God is perfectly holy and just (Isaiah 6:3), and because every human being has sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23), we are naturally separated from Him and cannot enter heaven by our own efforts or moral goodness. However, in His great love, God the Son took on human nature, lived a perfect life of obedience, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord over all.

Salvation and the promise of heaven are gifts received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What holds you back from trusting Him today? 

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible, which beautifully records the words and works of Jesus, so that you may know the certainty of the eternal life He offers (John 20:31). If you are able, seek out a local, Bible-teaching church where you can hear the Word of God preached and find fellowship with other believers.

question: Does the Bible teach geocentrism?
answer:
The Bible does not explicitly teach geocentrism as a scientific or astronomical model of the solar system. Instead, the biblical writers frequently use phenomenological language, which is the language of appearance, describing events from the perspective of a human observer on earth.

When the Scriptures speak of the sun rising or setting, as seen in Psalm 19:4–6, they are describing the visible reality of the day. In the book of Joshua, we read the historical account of the sun and moon standing still at God's command so that Israel could complete their victory. Joshua 10:12–13 states, "Sun, stand still over Gibeon, and moon, over the Valley of Aijalon. So the sun stood still and the moon stopped." This was a miraculous, historical intervention by the Creator who has total authority over His creation. This description records what Joshua and the people saw from their vantage point, not a formal statement on celestial mechanics.

Similarly, when the Psalms speak of the earth being established so that it cannot be moved, such as in Psalm 93:1 and Psalm 104:5, the focus is on the providential stability and security God provides to His creation. These passages emphasize that the world is not chaotic or random but is held firmly by the hand of God.

While the Bible may not demand a geocentric physical model, it certainly presents a "theocentric" and "anthropocentric" view of history. The earth is the central stage of God’s redemptive work. It is here that God "so loved the world" that He sent His only Son to take on human flesh. John 3:16; John 1:14. The Incarnation, the crucifixion, and the bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ occurred on this planet, making it the focal point of God’s glory and the location where He is reconciling sinners to Himself.

For the believer, the primary concern is not the physical coordinates of the earth in space, but our spiritual standing before the Holy God who made the stars. You can trust that the Bible is the inerrant Word of God, perfectly sufficient to lead you to salvation and godliness. 2 Timothy 3:16–17.

As a practical step in your discipleship, I encourage you to read through Psalm 19 this week. As you do, pray and ask the Lord to help you see both His glory in the heavens and the perfection of His written Law in your heart.

If you find yourself wondering about the vastness of the universe and where you fit within it, remember that the One who counts the stars also knows the hairs on your head. Matthew 10:30. The most important "center" is that Christ remains at the center of your life and faith. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

question: Why did Jesus say, “I have not come to call the righteous but sinners to repentance” (Matthew 9:13)?
answer:
Jesus uttered these words to expose the self-righteousness of the Pharisees and to announce that His mission is a rescue operation for the brokenhearted.
