He declares that "as you have done, it shall be done to you; your reprisal will return upon your own head" according to Obadiah 1:15. This is a sobering reminder that while the wicked may seem secure in their arrogance, God sees every injustice and will surely bring all things to light.

The book is structured into two main movements:
1. The condemnation and certain downfall of Edom (Obadiah 1:1-16).
2. The future restoration of Israel and the ultimate victory of God’s kingdom (Obadiah 1:17-21).

In our own walk of faith, we are called to examine our hearts for any trace of the "Edomite" spirit, which manifests as prideful self-sufficiency or a secret delight in the failures of others. We must instead cultivate a spirit of humble compassion, remembering that we are entirely dependent on the mercy of God.

While Obadiah speaks clearly of judgment, it ends with a glorious note of hope. The final verse promises that "the kingdom will be the LORD's" in Obadiah 1:21. This points us forward to the ultimate Deliverer, Jesus Christ, who did not gloat over our fallen state but descended from His heavenly throne to take our judgment upon Himself. Through His death and bodily resurrection, He has conquered every enemy of His people. You are invited to turn from your sins today and trust in this victorious King, finding refuge in the grace that He alone provides. What might be keeping you from resting fully in His sovereign care?

question: What does it mean to gain the whole world but lose your soul?
answer:
To gain the whole world but lose your soul describes the ultimate and eternal tragedy of choosing temporary, earthly satisfaction over an eternal relationship with God. Our Lord Jesus Christ poses this question to highlight the infinite value of the human soul compared to the fleeting nature of material wealth, social status, or worldly power as seen in Matthew 16:26 and Mark 8:36.

The world and all its desires are passing away, but the soul is immortal, created by God to exist forever. When a person "gains the world," they may achieve every ambition and possess every luxury, yet these things cannot satisfy the deepest needs of the human spirit nor can they be carried beyond the grave. To "lose the soul" means to face eternal condemnation and separation from the presence of God due to sin, which is a debt that no amount of earthly riches can repay as noted in Psalm 49:7-8.

This teaching reveals the reality of our condition. Because of our sin, we are naturally inclined to seek life in the creation rather than the Creator. However, the Bible teaches that the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord as stated in Romans 6:23. This is the great exchange of the Gospel. Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, who possessed all things, became poor for our sake. He lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death.

True life is not found in what we can accumulate, but in being reconciled to God through the finished work of His Son. When we try to save our own lives by clutching the world, we lose them, but when we surrender our lives to Christ, we find eternal joy that the world can neither give nor take away.

If you find yourself chasing the shadows of this world while feeling a void in your spirit, I encourage you to consider the state of your soul before a holy and loving God. You can turn to Him now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

A practical next step for you today would be to set aside fifteen minutes this evening to read the Gospel of John, chapter 3, and honestly ask God in prayer to show you the true value of your soul and the sufficiency of His grace. What is it that currently feels most valuable to you, and how does it compare to the promise of eternity with Christ?

question: Why did God send the flood when He knew sin would continue after the flood?
answer:
God sent the flood to execute His righteous judgment upon a world that had become entirely corrupted by sin and violence, while simultaneously preserving a human remnant through whom He would fulfill His promise of a coming Savior. Genesis 6:11–13. Although God knew that the inclination of the human heart would remain evil after the waters receded, the flood served as a historical demonstration of His absolute holiness and His necessary opposition to sin.

We must understand that God is not a passive observer of evil. In the days of Noah, the wickedness of man was great on the earth and every intent of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually. Genesis 6:5. By sending the flood, God acted as a righteous Judge to stop the unchecked spread of depravity that threatened to extinguish the line of the promised seed who would eventually crush the serpent’s head. Genesis 3:15.

The fact that sin continued after the flood serves a profound theological purpose. It proves that an external washing of the earth cannot cure the internal corruption of the human soul. This condition, often called total depravity, means that our problem is not just our environment, but our very nature. Even after the flood, God acknowledged that the intent of man’s heart is evil from his youth, yet He promised never again to destroy every living thing with a flood. Genesis 8:21. This points us away from the water of the flood and toward the ultimate washing found in the blood of Christ.

The flood was a type, or a prophetic picture, of the final judgment to come, and the ark was a type of Jesus Christ. Just as Noah and his family were safe from the storm only because they were inside the ark, we are safe from the righteous wrath of God against sin only if we are found in Christ. God did not send the flood to perfect humanity through the judgment of water, but to preserve humanity until He could redeem His people through the sacrifice of His Son.

If you find yourself troubled by the reality of sin in the world or in your own heart, I encourage you to read through the account of God’s covenant with Noah in Genesis 9. As you do, reflect on how God’s patience is intended to lead us to repentance.

The truth we must all face is that God is holy and we are sinful, separated from Him by our transgressions. However, the Gospel is the good news that Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, took on human nature to save us. He lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works we can perform. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting Him for your salvation today?

question: Should a church tithe 10% of the offerings it receives?
answer:
The Bible does not provide a direct command requiring a local church to give exactly ten percent of its collected offerings to other organizations or missions. While the Old Testament required the Levites to give a tithe of the tithes they received to the high priest (Numbers 18:26), the New Testament focuses on the principles of heart-felt generosity, wise stewardship, and the support of the Gospel ministry.

In the New Covenant, the emphasis shifts from a legalistic percentage to the spiritual quality of our giving. The Apostle Paul instructs that "Each one should give what he has decided in his heart to give, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver" (2 Corinthians 9:7). This principle applies to the individual believer, but it also serves as a beautiful pattern for the local church as a corporate body.

There are several biblical reasons why a church might choose to set aside a portion of its budget for external ministry:

First, it models the very generosity that the elders and pastors ask of the congregation. When a church prioritizes giving to missions, church planting, or the relief of the poor, it demonstrates that its primary mission is the advancement of the Kingdom of God rather than the mere maintenance of its own facilities or programs. We see this spirit in the early church, where believers in Macedonia gave "beyond their ability" to support the saints in Jerusalem (2 Corinthians 8:3).

Second, it fulfills the Great Commission. The church is called to make disciples of all nations (Matthew 28:19-20). Often, a local congregation fulfills this by partnering with missionaries and other faithful ministries. By allocating a percentage of its budget, the church ensures that the work of the Gospel continues in places where the local members cannot physically go (Philippians 4:15-16).

Third, it acknowledges God’s ownership. Everything the church receives belongs to the Lord (Psalm 24:1). Setting aside a portion of the church's income for others is a corporate act of worship that declares, "Lord, we trust You to provide for our needs as we seek to care for the needs of others."

Whether a church chooses ten percent, or a different amount, is a matter of Christian liberty and the wisdom of the elders. It is a tertiary issue where faithful brothers and sisters may differ in practice based on the specific needs and calling of their local context. The goal should always be to use the resources God has entrusted to the church for His glory and the good of His people.

If you are a member of a local church, a helpful next step would be to pray for your elders as they manage the church's finances. You might also ask to see the church's budget or attend a members' meeting to learn how your congregation currently supports the spread of the Gospel outside your own walls. This is a wonderful way to grow in your understanding of how the body of Christ works together to steward God's provision.

question: Easter Bible reading plan
answer:
An Easter Bible reading plan typically follows the final week of the earthly ministry of Jesus, often called Holy Week, focusing on His journey from the Triumphal Entry to the empty tomb. This chronological approach allows you to meditate on the specific events that accomplished our redemption through the life, death, and resurrection of our Lord.

Here is an eight day plan to guide your study:

Day 1: The Triumphal Entry. Read Matthew 21:1-11. Behold your King arriving in humility to fulfill the ancient promises of God.

Day 2: Authority and Cleansing. Read Mark 11:12-19. Observe the zeal of Christ for the holiness of His Father’s house.

Day 3: Wisdom and Warning. Read Matthew 22:15-40. Listen to the Great Physician as He silences His critics with the perfect truth of Scripture.

Day 4: The Anointing and the Betrayal. Read Matthew 26:1-16. Contrast the beautiful devotion of the woman who anointed Jesus with the darkness of Judas’s treachery.

Day 5: The Last Supper and Gethsemane. Read John 13:1-17 and Matthew 26:36-46. See the Savior’s servant heart and His agonizing submission to the Father’s will in the garden.

Day 6: The Trial and Crucifixion. Read John 19:16-37. Meditate on the mystery of substitutionary atonement, where the spotless Lamb of God bore the wrath we deserved to reconcile us to the Father.

Day 7: The Burial and the Watch. Read Matthew 27:57-66. Reflect on the silence of the tomb and the reality that Christ truly died for our sins.

Day 8: The Resurrection. Read John 20:1-18. Rejoice in the historical fact of the empty tomb, which confirms Christ’s victory over death and our justification before God.

The heart of the Christian faith is found in these historical events. Because God is perfectly holy and we have all rebelled against Him through sin, we were rightfully under His judgment. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a sacrificial death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. This salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works of our own. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

As a practical step for your discipleship, I encourage you to set aside fifteen minutes each morning this week to read the assigned passage slowly. After reading, ask the Lord in prayer to show you more of the beauty of His character and the depth of His love revealed in the cross. What is one specific truth about Jesus from these readings that you find most comforting today?

question: What is ignosticism? What is an ignostic?
answer:
Ignosticism is the philosophical view that the existence of God is a meaningless question because the term "God" has no coherent or unambiguous definition. An ignostic is a person who maintains that every theological claim is intellectually empty until a clear, verifiable definition of the divine is established.

While an atheist denies the existence of God and an agnostically minded person claims that God’s existence is unknown or unknowable, the ignostic argues that the very concept of God is "non-cognitive." This means they believe religious language fails to convey actual facts or meaning. They treat the word "God" as a sound without a specific reference, much like a nonsense word, and therefore they refuse to even debate whether such a being exists until the terms are defined to their satisfaction.

From a biblical perspective, however, God is not an undefined or abstract idea, but the living and sovereign Creator who has purposefully revealed Himself to humanity. Scripture teaches that God has provided "general revelation" through the physical world, so that His eternal power and divine nature are clearly seen by all people (Romans 1:19-20). Furthermore, He has provided "special revelation" through His written Word and most supremely through the person of Jesus Christ. 

In the Gospel of John, Jesus is called the "Word," which in the original language is *Logos*, meaning the logic or the expression of God (John 1:1). Rather than leaving us in a state of linguistic confusion, God became flesh and dwelt among us so that we could truly know Him (John 1:14). While God is "incomprehensible" in the sense that we cannot know Him exhaustively, He is truly "knowable" because He has spoken. He has defined Himself as holy, just, and loving (Exodus 34:6-7; 1 John 4:8).

You may wonder how a finite human mind can truly grasp an infinite God, and it is true that our language often falls short of His glory. Yet, the Bible tells us that our primary problem is not a lack of definitions, but a separation from God caused by our sin. Because God is holy and we have all turned away from His ways, we are in need of reconciliation (Isaiah 59:2; Romans 3:23). 

The good news is that Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is not found in solving a philosophical puzzle, but is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

If you find yourself questioning who God is or if He can be known, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John and ask God to reveal the truth of Jesus to your heart. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him today?

question: What does it mean that Jesus is the Lord of the Sabbath?
answer:
Jesus being the Lord of the Sabbath means that He possesses sovereign authority over the day of rest, as He is the Creator who instituted it and the Lawgiver who defines its true intent. This declaration, found in Matthew 12:8, Mark 2:28, and Luke 6:5, is a profound assertion of His deity, for only God can claim ownership and authority over a day that He Himself set apart as holy.

When the Pharisees accused the disciples of breaking the law by picking grain to eat on the Sabbath, Jesus corrected their narrow legalism. He reminded them that the Sabbath was created as a gift for humanity's benefit and refreshment, not as a rigid burden to enslave the conscience (Mark 2:27). By calling Himself Lord of the Sabbath, Jesus was teaching that the day points ultimately to Him. He is the substance, while the Old Testament Sabbath regulations were a shadow pointing to the reality found in Christ (Colossians 2:16; Colossians 2:17).

This truth has significant practical implications for our faith. It means that our spiritual rest is not found in the mere cessation of physical labor, but in the person and finished work of Jesus Christ. He is the one who provides the true rest for our souls that the weekly Sabbath could only symbolize (Matthew 11:28; Matthew 11:29; Matthew 11:30). In His life, death, and resurrection, He has satisfied the requirements of God's law on our behalf, bringing us into a state of permanent peace with God (Hebrews 4:9; Hebrews 4:10).

Dear friend, this is the heart of the Gospel. We are all prone to wander and burdened by the guilt of our sin, which separates us from a holy and just God. Yet, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the death we deserved. He was buried and rose again bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. We are justified before God not by our own works or religious observances, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God even now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. Is there anything preventing you from letting go of your own efforts and resting entirely in the mercy of the Savior?

As a step of discipleship, I encourage you to read through Hebrews chapter 3 and Hebrews chapter 4 this week. As you read, pray that the Holy Spirit would show you how to cease from your own works and rest securely in the grace of the Lord of the Sabbath.

question: What does it mean that Jesus Christ conquered death?
answer:
Jesus Christ conquered death by physically and literally rising from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His absolute authority over the power of the grave and the consequences of sin. This victory means that death, which was once an inescapable prison for humanity, has been transformed into a defeated foe for all who belong to Him (1 Corinthians 15:54–57).

To understand this victory, we must first recognize why death held power over us. Scripture teaches that God is perfectly holy and just, and death entered the world as the rightful penalty for human sin (Romans 5:12; Romans 6:23). Because every person has rebelled against God, we all face the sentence of physical death and eternal separation from Him.

However, in His great mercy, God the Son took on human nature in the person of Jesus Christ. He lived a life of perfect obedience, fulfilling the requirements of God's law that we could never meet. On the cross, Jesus offered Himself as a substitutionary sacrifice, bearing the full weight of God’s righteous wrath against our sin. When He died, He paid our debt in full. When He rose again, He proved that His sacrifice was accepted and that the power of sin had been broken (Romans 4:25).

Because Christ is risen, He is the "firstfruits" of those who have fallen asleep, serving as the guarantee that those who trust in Him will also be raised to eternal life (1 Corinthians 15:20–23). He now sits at the right hand of the Father, having stripped death of its sting and the grave of its victory. He holds the keys of Death and Hades, sovereign over every soul and the final destiny of all creation (Revelation 1:18).

For the believer, this means that death is no longer something to be feared as a final end or a judgment. Instead, it is a doorway into the immediate presence of the Savior. This hope is not a vague wish but is grounded in the historical fact of the empty tomb.

The Gospel is the good news that although we deserve death, God offers us life. This salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him as your Savior and Lord?

As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to meditate on the words of Jesus in John 11:25–26 and bring your fears of mortality to Him in prayer, asking Him to strengthen your faith in His finished work. Seek fellowship with a local church where the Word of God is faithfully preached, so you may be encouraged by the community of those who share this living hope.

question: What are the books of the Bible? What does it mean that the Bible is composed of different books?
answer:
The Bible is a single, unified library consisting of 66 individual books, divided into two main sections: the Old Testament, which contains 39 books, and the New Testament, which contains 27 books. These books were written by approximately 40 different authors over a period of about 1,500 years, yet they speak with one harmonious voice because they are the inspired Word of the living God.

The Old Testament books begin with the Pentateuch (Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy), followed by the Historical books (Joshua, Judges, Ruth, 1 Samuel, 2 Samuel, 1 Kings, 2 Kings, 1 Chronicles, 2 Chronicles, Ezra, Nehemiah, and Esther). Next are the Poetic and Wisdom books (Job, Psalms, Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, and Song of Solomon), and finally the Prophetic books, which include the Major Prophets (Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, and Daniel) and the twelve Minor Prophets (Hosea through Malachi).

The New Testament opens with the four Gospels (Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John), which record the life, death, and resurrection of our Lord Jesus Christ. These are followed by the book of Acts (history of the early church), the Pauline Epistles (Romans through Philemon), the General Epistles (Hebrews through Jude), and the book of Revelation.

That the Bible is composed of different books means that God, in His infinite wisdom, chose the method of organic inspiration to reveal Himself to us. This theological truth signifies that the Holy Spirit so moved and directed the human authors that, while they used their own distinct personalities, vocabularies, and literary styles, the resulting text is exactly what God intended to say, down to the very words. We call this plenary verbal inspiration, meaning the Bible is entirely God breathed in all its parts. 2 Timothy 3:16; 2 Peter 1:20-21.

This diversity of books serves several beautiful purposes:

First, it demonstrates the "progressive revelation" of God’s plan. Like the light of dawn growing brighter until the full sun rises, God revealed His purposes in stages, beginning with the promises in the garden of Eden and culminating in the person of Jesus Christ. Hebrews 1:1-2.

Second, the variety of genres—such as history, poetry, and letters—allows the Word of God to meet us in every season of life. Whether we are mourning with the Psalmists, seeking wisdom in the Proverbs, or being corrected by the Epistles, the Scriptures are sufficient for all of life and godliness. 

Third, the miraculous unity among these different books proves their divine origin. Though written by kings, fishermen, and scholars across three continents and in three languages (Hebrew, Aramaic, and Greek), every book points toward the same central theme: the glory of God in the redemption of sinners through the substitutionary sacrifice of Jesus Christ. Luke 24:27; John 5:39.

Beloved, I encourage you to approach these books not merely as ancient literature, but as the very voice of your Father speaking to you today. A wonderful next step would be to begin reading the Gospel of John. As you read, ask the Holy Spirit to open your eyes to see the beauty of Christ, for it is through this Word that we find eternal life. John 20:31.

question: What is the creation mandate / cultural mandate?
answer:
The creation mandate, often called the cultural mandate, is God’s original instruction to humanity to flourish, multiply, and exercise wise stewardship over the earth as His representatives. This foundational commission is recorded in the opening chapter of Scripture, where God blessed the first man and woman and commanded them to fill the earth and subdue it (Genesis 1:28).

At the heart of this mandate is the reality of the Imago Dei, or the Image of God. Because God created human beings in His own image, we are uniquely called to reflect His character and creativity within the world He made (Genesis 1:26; Genesis 1:27). This is not a license for the selfish exploitation of nature, but a high calling to care for the creation as a gardener tends a garden, bringing order out of chaos and beauty out of the raw materials of the earth (Genesis 2:15). 

This mandate remains in effect today, though it has been complicated by the entrance of sin into the world. While the Fall brought thorns, thistles, and toil to our labor, it did not revoke our purpose (Genesis 3:17; Genesis 3:18; Genesis 3:19). In a biblical worldview, there is no sacred-secular divide in our activities. Every honest labor, whether it is raising a family, painting a canvas, practicing medicine, or managing a business, is an opportunity to honor God by fulfilling this mandate. By developing culture, technology, and art, we testify to the glory of the Great Creator.

For the believer, the creation mandate is now lived out in light of the Great Commission. While we continue to steward the physical world, we do so while pointing others to the restoration found in Jesus Christ, who is the perfect Image of the invisible God (Colossians 1:15; Matthew 28:18; Matthew 28:19; Matthew 28:20). We work not merely for our own survival or success, but as unto the Lord, knowing that our labor in Him is never in vain (Colossians 3:23; Colossians 3:24; 1 Corinthians 15:58).

Beloved, I encourage you to see your daily responsibilities this week as a holy calling. Whether your hands are on a keyboard or a steering wheel, you are serving as God’s steward in His world.

As an actionable step in your discipleship, take a moment today to offer your specific work or chores to God in prayer. Ask Him to help you perform your tasks with excellence and a joyful heart, specifically looking for one way you can bring more order, beauty, or kindness to your environment as a reflection of His character.

question: What does it mean that friendship with the world is enmity with God (James 4:4)?
answer:
Friendship with the world means adopting the values, priorities, and rebellious spirit of a human society that lives in independence from and opposition to God. To be a friend of the world in the sense James describes is to align your heart’s desires with a system that rejects Christ’s lordship, which places a person in a state of active hostility or enmity toward God.

James uses the piercing term "adulteresses" in James 4:4 to remind us that for a believer, the relationship with God is a sacred covenant, much like a marriage. When we seek satisfaction, identity, or moral guidance from the world's fallen system, we are committing spiritual adultery. The "world" here does not refer to the physical earth or the people in it—whom we are commanded to love—but rather the organized system of pride, self-sufficiency, and sinful desire that dominates human culture. As the Apostle John warns, this world system consists of the lust of the flesh, the lust of the eyes, and the pride of life, none of which comes from the Father (1 John 2:15-16).

This enmity exists because the world and the Kingdom of God are governed by two fundamentally different sets of principles. The world seeks to exalt the self, while God’s Kingdom seeks to exalt the Savior. The world operates on the basis of temporary sight and immediate gratification, whereas the believer is called to live by faith in the eternal (2 Corinthians 5:7). You cannot walk in two opposite directions at the same time; therefore, to choose the world’s way is to turn your back on God’s way. Jesus Himself taught that no one can serve two masters, for he will either hate the one and love the other, or be devoted to the one and despise the other (Matthew 6:24).

This is a sobering call to the necessity of sanctification, which is the Spirit-led process of being set apart from sin and the world's influence to be made holy for God’s use. We are in the world, but we must not be of it (John 17:14-16). This requires a daily renewal of our minds so that we are no longer conformed to the patterns of this age (Romans 12:2). 

If you find that your heart is increasingly drawn to the approval and the ways of this world, remember that Christ died to deliver us from this present evil age (Galatians 1:4). Our natural state is one of rebellion and enmity, but God, who is rich in mercy, offers reconciliation through His Son. Jesus Christ lived a perfectly holy life, died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins—including our spiritual unfaithfulness—and rose from the dead to give us new life.

You can turn to God today by repenting of your sins and trusting in Jesus Christ alone for your salvation. He is a faithful Husband to His church and a gracious Savior to all who call upon Him.

As a practical step for your spiritual growth, I encourage you to sit quietly this week and examine your "loves." Ask yourself: Do my entertainment choices, my use of money, and my conversations look indistinguishable from those who do not know God? Bring these areas to Him in prayer, asking the Holy Spirit to increase your love for Christ so that the things of this world naturally lose their appeal.

question: Why did God harden Pharaoh’s heart?
answer:
God hardened Pharaoh’s heart to display His supreme power over the false gods of Egypt and to ensure that His name would be proclaimed throughout all the earth. This act was a judicial judgment, where God confirmed Pharaoh in the stubbornness that the king had already chosen for himself.

In the biblical account, we see a profound interplay between the sovereignty of God and human responsibility. In the early stages of the plagues, the Scriptures record that Pharaoh hardened his own heart, as seen in Exodus 8:15 and Exodus 8:32. Eventually, God gave Pharaoh over to his own rebellion. This is often referred to as judicial hardening. It means that God withdrew His restraining grace and allowed Pharaoh’s natural depravity to run its full, ruinous course. God used this to accomplish a specific redemptive purpose, as Exodus 9:16 explains: "But I have raised you up for this very purpose, that I might show you My power and that My name might be proclaimed in all the earth."

The Apostle Paul reinforces this truth in the New Testament, stating that God has mercy on whom He desires, and He hardens whom He desires, in Romans 9:17–18. This teaches us that God is the supreme King who is never a passive observer of history. Even the pride and wrath of man are redirected by His hand to bring Him praise and to accomplish His holy will.

There is a sobering pastoral lesson here. The same sun that melts the wax also hardens the clay. When we are confronted with the light of God’s Word and refuse to submit, our hearts can become calloused and resistant. We are all naturally inclined toward this same hardness of heart because of our sin, which separates us from our holy and just Creator.

However, the glory of the Gospel is that God provides a way for the hard heart to be made new. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, came to earth, lived a perfect life of obedience, and died a substitutionary death on the cross for sinners. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He now offers forgiveness and a new heart to all who turn to Him.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As an actionable step, I encourage you to read through the first fourteen chapters of the book of Exodus. Pay close attention to the greatness of God’s power and His faithfulness to deliver His people. As you read, talk to God directly and ask Him to soften your heart and give you a deeper understanding of His holiness and grace.

question: How should we treat our enemies according to Proverbs 25:21–22?
answer:
According to Proverbs 25:21–22, we are commanded to treat our enemies with practical, sacrificial kindness by meeting their most basic needs. The passage instructs us that if an enemy is hungry, we should give him food, and if he is thirsty, we should give him water (Proverbs 25:21).

This response to hostility is not merely a passive avoidance of conflict, but an active pursuit of their well being. By showing mercy instead of seeking vengeance, you heap burning coals on his head (Proverbs 25:22). In the context of biblical wisdom, this imagery refers to the fire of conviction or a sense of shame that our kindness can ignite in an opponent's heart, potentially leading them toward repentance. It is a way of overcoming evil with good, trusting that the Lord is the ultimate judge who sees your heart and will reward those who walk in obedience to His word (Romans 12:20; Romans 12:21).

Beloved, this command is a beautiful reflection of the Gospel itself. The Bible teaches that while we were still sinners and enemies of God, Christ died for us to reconcile us to the Father (Romans 5:8; Romans 5:10). We do not earn God's favor by our own efforts, but because we have been justified by grace through faith in Jesus Christ, we are now being transformed in our sanctification to love as He loved.

As a practical step this week, identify one person who has caused you pain or remains in opposition to you. Instead of dwelling on the hurt or withdrawing in bitterness, find one tangible way to serve them, perhaps through a helpful gesture or a small act of generosity. In doing so, you mirror the heart of your Savior.

If you find your heart burdened by the weight of your own sins or the difficulty of forgiving others, remember that Jesus Christ took the judgment we deserved upon Himself on the cross. He rose again to give new life and a new heart to all who trust in Him. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

question: What does the Bible say about indecision / being indecisive?
answer:
The Bible describes chronic indecision as being double-minded, a state where a person's soul is divided and unstable in all their ways (James 1:8). It characterizes the indecisive person as being like a wave of the sea, driven and tossed by the wind, lacking the firm anchor of faith (James 1:6).

True decisiveness is rooted in a settled trust in the sovereignty and providence of God. Often, our inability to choose stems from a fear of the future or a desire to please men rather than God (Proverbs 29:25). Theologically, we must remember that God is the supreme governor of all things, and He has promised to give wisdom generously to those who ask Him in faith without wavering (James 1:5). In the history of Israel, the prophet Elijah confronted the people for their hesitation, asking how long they would waver between two opinions and calling them to a definitive choice for the Lord (1 Kings 18:21). We are not called to a life of paralysis, but to a life of bold obedience, trusting that as we acknowledge Him, He will make our paths straight (Proverbs 3:5-6).

If you are currently struggling with a difficult decision, a practical next step is to set aside twenty minutes today to read through Psalm 25. Use it as a guide for your own heart, asking the Lord to show you His ways and teach you His paths. After this, speak with a godly mentor or an elder in your local church to seek wise counsel, as there is safety in the abundance of counselors (Proverbs 11:14).

The most dangerous form of indecision is found in our response to the Lord Jesus Christ. To remain undecided about Christ is, in truth, a decision to remain in your sins and under the judgment of God. The Bible teaches that God is holy and just, yet we have all rebelled against Him. Out of His great mercy, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, took on human flesh through the virgin birth and lived a perfect life. He died on the cross as a substitutionary sacrifice for sinners, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. He is now the exalted Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works of our own. You can turn to God today, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him at this moment?

question: What does it mean that God will wipe away every tear (Revelation 21:4)?
answer:
The promise that God will wipe away every tear signifies the complete and final removal of all sorrow, suffering, and the very causes of grief in the coming new creation. This is not merely a gesture of comfort, but a sovereign act where God abolishes the "former things," which include death, mourning, and pain (Revelation 21:4).

In our current experience, tears are the natural response to the brokenness and decay caused by sin. However, for the believer, the Bible teaches that our present afflictions are preparing for us an eternal weight of glory that far outweighs them all (2 Corinthians 4:17). The "wiping away" means that the sting of death is gone and the curse of the Fall is reversed (Revelation 22:3). This is the great consummation of our salvation, where God’s presence is so direct and His restoration so thorough that the memory of earthly pain is swallowed up by the overwhelming joy of His glory (Isaiah 25:8).

If you are walking through a season of deep sorrow or what the Psalmist calls "the valley of the shadow of death," please take heart (Psalm 23:4). This verse is a historical guarantee from your Creator that your pain has an expiration date. Your tears are precious to Him, and He keeps count of them now (Psalm 56:8), but a day is coming when they will be entirely unnecessary because the root of all sadness, which is sin and separation from God, will be destroyed.

This glorious inheritance belongs to those who have been reconciled to God through Jesus Christ. We must recognize that God is perfectly holy, and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Yet, God demonstrates His love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Romans 5:8). Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human flesh, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy God’s justice on our behalf. He rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your discipleship, I encourage you to read through Revelation chapter 21 and Revelation chapter 22 this week. In your prayers, honestly bring your current hurts to God, asking Him to help you fix your eyes on the eternal hope that is secured for you in Christ. Seek fellowship with a local body of believers who can walk with you and remind you of these truths during difficult times.

question: What is the meaning of “there but for the grace of God go I”?
answer:
The phrase "there but for the grace of God go I" is a humble acknowledgment that every person is capable of falling into the same sins or suffering the same calamities as others, were it not for the intervening mercy of God. It expresses the profound truth that our standing and our safety are not the results of our own merits, superior wisdom, or inherent goodness, but are entirely dependent on God's sovereign favor.

This sentiment is deeply rooted in the biblical understanding of our human condition. Apart from the work of the Holy Spirit, the human heart is naturally prone to wander and rebel against its Creator. The Apostle Paul captures this reality when he asks, "For who makes you different from anyone else? What do you have that you did not receive? And if you did receive it, why do you boast as though you did not?" (1 Corinthians 4:7). Any goodness we possess or any evil we have avoided is a result of God's restraining grace, which keeps us from the full expression of our fallen nature (Romans 3:10-12).

Theologically, this perspective points to the doctrines of total depravity and justification. We recognize that "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23), and it is only by being "justified freely by His grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus" (Romans 3:24) that we are saved. When we see a fellow human being in a state of spiritual or physical ruin, this perspective replaces judgmental pride with Christlike compassion. It reminds us that believers are kept only by the power of God through faith (1 Peter 1:5).

The ultimate expression of this truth is found in the Gospel. God is holy and just, and our sin has separated us from Him, leaving us deserving of His righteous judgment. Yet, in His great love, Jesus Christ took on human nature, lived a perfect and sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins. He was buried and rose again bodily on the third day, conquering sin and death. Salvation is a gift of God, received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8-9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As a practical step in your walk with the Lord, I suggest that the next time you witness someone struggling with the consequences of sin or hardship, stop and pray for them immediately. Use that moment to thank God for His specific mercies in your own life and ask Him to provide you with an opportunity to serve that person or share the hope of Christ with them in gentleness and respect (1 Peter 3:15).

question: How can we walk in God’s ways (Psalm 128:1)?
answer:
Beloved, walking in God’s ways is the practical outworking of a heart that has been captured by His grace and transformed by His Spirit. To walk in His ways is to align our will, our affections, and our actions with the revealed truth of the Bible, finding our greatest joy in pleasing the Father (Psalm 128:1). This path begins with the fear of the Lord, which is not a trembling dread of punishment for the believer, but a profound, holy awe and a delight in His majestic character (Proverbs 9:10).

To walk in His ways effectively, we must look to several biblical pillars:

First, we must love Him. Jesus taught that if we love Him, we will keep His commandments (John 14:15). Obedience is not the engine of our salvation, but it is the evidence of it. We do not walk to get to God; we walk because He has already reached out and rescued us. This is the beauty of the Gospel, that while we were still sinners and wandering in our own ways, Christ died for us.

Second, we must depend on the Holy Spirit. In our own strength, we are prone to wander. God promised in the New Covenant to put His Spirit within us to cause us to walk in His statutes (Ezekiel 36:27). This process is known as progressive sanctification, where the Spirit gradually conforms our character to the image of Jesus Christ. It is His grace that trains us to renounce ungodliness and to live self controlled, upright, and godly lives in this present age (Titus 2:11-12).

Third, we must abide in the Word. The Bible is the lamp to our feet and the light to our path (Psalm 119:105). We cannot walk in ways we do not know. By soaking our minds in Scripture, we learn the heart of God and the standards of His kingdom.

If you find that you have strayed from His path or feel the weight of your own inability to be perfect, remember that Jesus Christ is the only one who ever walked in God's ways perfectly. He is the Way, the Truth, and the Life (John 14:6). Through His sacrificial death and bodily resurrection, He has cleared the way for us to be reconciled to God. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone, not by our own works or walking. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and the power to walk anew.

As a practical next step in your discipleship, I encourage you to begin each morning this week by reading one Psalm and asking the Lord in prayer, "Father, show me Your character in these words and help me to walk in a way that honors You today." What is one area of your life where you find it most difficult to trust His direction?

question: What does the Bible say about the ice age?
answer:
The Bible does not use the modern term ice age, but it provides the historical framework for such a period as a direct consequence of the Great Flood in the days of Noah. In the biblical record, the upheaval of the earth during the Flood involved the breaking open of the fountains of the great deep, which would have resulted in significantly warmer oceans due to volcanic and tectonic activity.

Genesis 7:11; Genesis 8:2.

This unique set of conditions—warm oceans providing high evaporation and volcanic ash in the atmosphere cooling the land—created the perfect environment for the rapid accumulation of snow and the formation of massive ice sheets. While secular timelines suggest multiple ice ages over millions of years, the biblical narrative points to a single, major glacial period that occurred shortly after the Flood and likely lasted for several centuries.

The book of Job, which many scholars believe was written during the patriarchal period following the Flood, contains more references to ice, snow, and cold than any other book of the Bible. This suggests that the climate of the ancient Near East may have been significantly different during that time. God asks Job, "From whose womb comes the ice? And who gives birth to the frost of heaven? The waters harden like stone, and the surface of the deep is frozen."

Job 38:29–30; Job 37:10.

From a theological perspective, the Ice Age demonstrates the sovereign power of God over His creation and the lasting physical effects of His judgment upon sin. The Flood was a real historical event, a display of God's holy wrath, yet it was also an occasion where He showed mercy by preserving a remnant through the ark.

Just as the ark was the only place of safety during the Flood, Jesus Christ is the only refuge for us today. The Bible teaches that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, but salvation is found in Christ alone through His death and resurrection.

Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23.

You can trust the reliability of God's Word from the very first verse. I encourage you to read the account of the Flood in Genesis chapters 6 through 9 and reflect on how God provides a way of escape even in the midst of judgment. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. Have you ever considered how the historical truth of the Bible impacts your trust in God's promises for your own life?

question: What is the significance of the city of Nineveh in the Bible?
answer:
Nineveh was the magnificent and formidable capital of the Assyrian Empire, situated on the eastern bank of the Tigris River in modern day Iraq. In the biblical narrative, it serves as a powerful historical monument to both the expansive reach of God’s mercy and the terrifying certainty of His divine wrath against unrepentant sin.

The city is most famous for the account of the prophet Jonah, whom God commanded to preach a message of warning to its inhabitants. Though Nineveh was a center of paganism and brutal violence, the entire city, from the king to the lowest servant, responded to Jonah’s message with profound repentance. The Bible records that when God saw their works and how they turned from their evil ways, He relented from the disaster He had threatened (Jonah 3:10). This event demonstrates God’s sovereignty over all nations and His willingness to extend grace to those outside the covenant community of Israel.

However, the spiritual renewal of Nineveh was not lasting. In later generations, the city returned to its pride, cruelty, and idolatry. The prophet Nahum was then sent to declare the final and irreversible judgment of the Lord upon the city. Nahum describes God as "jealous and avenging," a God who "will by no means leave the guilty unpunished" (Nahum 1:2-3). Nineveh was eventually destroyed in 612 B.C., just as the Word of God had spoken through His prophet.

The Lord Jesus Christ later used the history of Nineveh to confront the unbelief of His own generation. He declared that "the men of Nineveh will stand up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it; for they repented at the preaching of Jonah, and behold, something greater than Jonah is here" (Matthew 12:41). By this, Jesus emphasized that greater light brings greater responsibility, and that the only proper response to the proclamation of the Gospel is immediate and humble faith.

The account of Nineveh teaches us that no person or nation is so wicked that they are beyond the reach of God's grace if they will truly repent, yet no one is so powerful that they can escape His justice if they persist in rebellion. It reminds us of our own need for the substitutionary work of Christ, who took the wrath we deserved so that we might receive a mercy even greater than that shown to the Ninevites.

As an actionable step in your own walk with God, I encourage you to set aside time today to examine your own heart before the Lord. If there is any known sin you have been harboring, turn to Him in prayer, confessing that sin specifically and resting in the promise that He is faithful and just to forgive those who trust in Jesus Christ (1 John 1:9). Seek the Lord while He may be found, and find your peace in His finished work on the cross.

question: What is Epicureanism? What did Epicurus teach?
answer:
Epicureanism is an ancient Greek system of philosophy founded by Epicurus around 307 BC that teaches the greatest good is to seek modest, sustainable pleasure in the form of a state of tranquility and freedom from fear and bodily pain. While often misunderstood as a license for gluttony or debauchery, Epicurus actually advocated for a simple life, teaching that the highest pleasure was found in the absence of pain and mental disturbance, a state he called ataraxia.

Epicurus based his teachings on a worldview of materialism, believing that the entire universe was composed of tiny, indivisible atoms and void. He taught that the soul was also material and perished with the body, leading to the conclusion that death is simply the end of consciousness and therefore should not be feared. In his view, gods might exist, but they are entirely uninvolved in human affairs and do not judge or interact with the world. Consequently, he encouraged his followers to live for the present, seeking to maximize peace and minimize suffering through friendship and the avoidance of political or stressful entanglements.

This philosophy directly confronted the early Church, most notably when the Apostle Paul encountered Epicurean and Stoic philosophers in Athens as recorded in Acts 17:18. The Bible presents a starkly different reality than the materialist boundaries of Epicureanism. Scripture teaches that God is not distant or uninvolved but is the sovereign Creator who sustains all things and will one day judge the world in righteousness by the Man He has appointed, Jesus Christ, as noted in Acts 17:31.

The Epicurean sought peace through the avoidance of pain, but the Christian finds peace through reconciliation with God. While Epicurus said that death is the end, the historical fact of the resurrection of Jesus Christ proves that there is life beyond the grave and a coming judgment. Jesus said in John 11:25, I am the resurrection and the life. He who believes in Me will live, even though he dies.

You may find yourself seeking the same tranquility that Epicurus sought, perhaps hoping that by avoiding trouble or pursuing comfort, you can find rest for your soul. However, true peace cannot be found in the absence of pain or the denial of the afterlife. It is found only in the person of Jesus Christ, who took our sins upon Himself on the cross to give us a peace that surpasses all understanding.

God is holy and just, and our sins have separated us from Him, but in His great love, He sent His Son to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death in our place. He rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. I encourage you to read the account of Paul in Athens in Acts 17 and consider the claims of Christ. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What do you find most appealing or perhaps most troubling about the idea that there is a life and a judgment after death?

question: Book of Philippians - Bible Survey
answer:
The Book of Philippians is a deeply personal and joyous epistle written by the Apostle Paul from prison to the believers in the city of Philippi. It serves as a warm thank you letter for the church's financial support and a profound exhortation to maintain Christian unity through the humility of Christ.

The church at Philippi was the first one established by Paul in Europe, as recorded in Acts 16:11-40. Though Paul was in chains while writing, likely in Rome, the letter is permeated with the theme of joy. He teaches that a believer's circumstances do not dictate their spiritual state because our life is hidden in Christ. Paul famously declares that to live is Christ and to die is gain, as found in Philippians 1:21.

A central theological pillar of this book is the Christ Hymn in Philippians 2:5-11. Here, Paul describes the *incarnation* and the *humility* of Jesus, who, being in the form of God, did not consider equality with God something to be grasped but emptied Himself by taking the form of a servant. This passage highlights the *hypostatic union*, the reality that Jesus is truly God and truly man, and emphasizes His obedience to the point of death on a cross for our sake.

Paul also addresses the vital doctrine of *justification* by faith. He warns against those who rely on legalistic rituals or their own merit, asserting that his own righteousness comes through faith in Christ alone, as stated in Philippians 3:9. Furthermore, he points to the reality of *sanctification*, assuring us that He who began a good work in us will bring it to completion at the day of Jesus Christ, according to Philippians 1:6. 

For the believer today, Philippians offers a roadmap for the "mind of Christ." It calls us to look out for the interests of others and to find our strength not in our own resolve, but in the Savior. Paul reminds us that our citizenship is in heaven, and we await a Savior from there who will transform our lowly bodies to be like His glorious body, as noted in Philippians 3:20-21.

As a practical step in your walk with the Lord, I encourage you to practice the "right thinking" described in Philippians 4:8. This week, when you feel overwhelmed by the world, purposefully dwell on things that are true, honorable, just, pure, lovely, and commendable. Bring your specific anxieties to God in prayer with thanksgiving, and ask Him to let His peace, which surpasses all understanding, guard your heart and mind in Christ Jesus, as promised in Philippians 4:6-7.

question: In what ways was Moses like Jesus?
answer:
Moses was a "type" of Christ, which means his life, office, and actions were sovereignly designed by God to serve as a prophetic shadow or architectural sketch of the coming Savior. This connection is rooted in the promise that God would raise up a prophet like Moses from among the people (Deuteronomy 18:15).

First, consider the role of a deliverer. Just as Moses was called by God to deliver the Israelites from the crushing physical bondage of Egypt (Exodus 12:51), Jesus Christ came to deliver His people from the far greater spiritual bondage of sin, Satan, and death (John 8:34–36; Colossians 1:13–14). Moses led the people through the Red Sea to physical freedom, but Jesus leads His people through the grave and into eternal life.

Second, Moses served as the mediator between a holy God and a rebellious people. He often stood in the gap to plead for mercy when the people deserved judgment (Exodus 32:30–32). In a much more perfect and final way, Jesus is the one Mediator between God and men (1 Timothy 2:5). He does not merely plead for mercy for us, He has satisfied the justice of God on our behalf through His substitutionary death on the cross, making a way for us to be reconciled to the Father (Romans 3:24–26).

Third, both were prophets who brought the Word of God to the people. Moses gave the Law at Mount Sinai, which revealed the holiness of God and the standard of righteousness. Jesus, however, is the very Word of God made flesh (John 1:1; John 1:14). While the Law was given through Moses, grace and truth came through Jesus Christ (John 1:17). Jesus did not come to abolish the Law Moses gave, but to fulfill it perfectly in our place so that we might be saved by His merit and not our own (Matthew 5:17).

While the similarities are striking, it is vital to recognize the superiority of Christ. Moses was a faithful servant in God’s house, but Jesus is the Son over the house (Hebrews 3:5–6). Moses could only point to the remedy for sin through animal sacrifices, but Jesus provided the final remedy through His own blood.

As a practical step in your discipleship, I encourage you to read the first four chapters of the book of Hebrews. This passage beautifully explains how Jesus is greater than Moses and why His sacrifice is our only sure foundation for peace with God.

If you feel the weight of your own failings today, do not look to your own ability to keep the Law, for the Law was given to show us our need for a Savior. Instead, look to Jesus, who died and rose to give you a righteousness you could never earn. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

question: What is Socinianism?
answer:
Socinianism is a nontrinitarian theological system that emerged in the 16th century, primarily through the influence of Faustus Socinus. It is defined by its denial of the pre-existence and deity of Jesus Christ, the doctrine of the Trinity, and the substitutionary nature of the atonement.

This system views Jesus as a mere man who, though born of a virgin and exceptionally holy, did not possess a divine nature. Socinians rejected the *hypostatic union*, which is the orthodox belief that Jesus is truly God and truly man in one person. They also argued against the necessity of Christ’s death as a satisfaction for divine justice, suggesting instead that His sacrifice was primarily a supreme example of perfect obedience for us to follow.

However, the Word of God provides a much more glorious and necessary picture of our Savior. Scripture teaches that in the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God (John 1:1). It further declares that Christ Himself bore our sins in His body on the tree, so that we might die to sin and live to righteousness (1 Peter 2:24). Without the infinite value of Christ's divinity, His death could not serve as a sufficient *propitiation* for the sins of the world. To deny the deity of the Son is to lose the Father as well (1 John 2:23; 2 John 1:9).

The true Gospel is that God, in His great love, sent His eternal Son to take on human flesh, live a perfect life, and die in the place of sinners to reconcile us to Himself. He rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. You can turn to God today, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone for your salvation and new life.

As a practical step for your own growth, I encourage you to read through the first chapter of the Gospel of John and the first chapter of Hebrews. These passages clearly lay out the eternal nature and supreme authority of Jesus, which will help anchor your heart in the truth of who He is.

question: Can an unmarried man be a deacon or elder?
answer:
Yes, a man who is not married may biblically serve as an elder or a deacon, provided he meets the other qualifications of character and giftedness set forth in the Word of God. The primary scriptural requirements for these offices are found in 1 Timothy 3:1–13 and Titus 1:5–9.

The phrase often cited in this discussion is that an overseer or deacon must be the husband of one wife (1 Timothy 3:2; 1 Timothy 3:12; Titus 1:6). In the original Greek, this literally means a one-woman man. The Apostle Paul is not mandating that every leader must be married, but rather that if a man is married, he must be absolutely faithful and devoted to his wife alone. This qualification excludes polygamists, those involved in sexual immorality, or those who are unfaithful in their hearts and conduct.

If marriage were a strict requirement for leadership, then the Apostle Paul himself, who was likely unmarried or a widower during his ministry, would have been disqualified from the very offices he was describing. Paul even speaks of the advantages of singleness in ministry, noting that the unmarried man is anxious about the things of the Lord, how he may please the Lord (1 Corinthians 7:32). Furthermore, our Lord Jesus Christ, the Chief Shepherd of the Church, remained unmarried during His earthly ministry.

While marriage and the faithful management of a household provide a proven testing ground for leadership (1 Timothy 3:4–5), a single man demonstrates his fitness through his self-control, his devotion to the local church, and his disciplined life. The focus of the Holy Spirit in these passages is not on a man's marital status, but on his moral integrity and spiritual maturity.

Within the history of the church, there have been some who interpreted these verses as requiring marriage, but the majority of faithful teachers recognize that this would create a standard that excludes even the apostles. We must be careful not to add to the commands of Scripture or to bind the consciences of brothers whom God has called to serve His people in a state of singleness.

If you are a single man considering the call to leadership, or if your church is evaluating a candidate, I encourage you to focus on the heart and the life. Seek a man who is above reproach, temperate, and able to teach (1 Timothy 3:2). Consider spending time in prayer and seeking the counsel of your local church elders to discern if the Lord is confirming this internal desire with the external recognition of the body. One practical step would be to study the lives of godly single men in church history to see how their devotion to Christ served the kingdom with undivided focus.

question: Is it better, spiritually, to be poor than to be rich?
answer:
Spiritually speaking, neither poverty nor wealth is inherently superior, as God looks upon the heart rather than the earthly ledger. The Bible teaches that both conditions carry specific spiritual opportunities and significant temptations, and our standing before God is determined by our relationship with Jesus Christ rather than our economic status.

Wealth is often described in Scripture as a blessing and a means of stewardship, yet it carries the grave danger of fostering self-sufficiency and idolatry. Jesus warned that it is difficult for a rich man to enter the kingdom of heaven because riches can easily become a false god (Matthew 19:23-24). The Apostle Paul further explains that the love of money, rather than money itself, is a root of all kinds of evil (1 Timothy 6:10). For the wealthy believer, the spiritual challenge is to remain humble and to practice radical generosity, recognizing that they are merely managers of God’s resources (1 Timothy 6:17-19).

Poverty, while often involving great suffering and hardship, can provide a unique path toward spiritual dependence. James 2:5 notes that God has chosen the poor in the eyes of the world to be rich in faith and to inherit the kingdom He promised to those who love Him. However, poverty also has its trials, such as the temptation to bitterness, despair, or the sin of theft to satisfy hunger (Proverbs 30:8-9). The spiritual goal for the poor is to find their worth and provision in the Lord, avoiding the trap of envy.

The biblical ideal is often found in the prayer of Agur, who asked God for "neither poverty nor riches," but only his daily bread, so that he would neither forget God through abundance nor dishonor God through desperate need (Proverbs 30:8-9). This reflects the virtue of contentment, which is the ability to rest in God’s sovereignty regardless of our circumstances (Philippians 4:11-13).

Ultimately, the greatest "wealth" is not found in a bank account but in the person of Jesus Christ. We must remember that Christ, though He was rich, for our sakes became poor, so that through His poverty we might become rich (2 Corinthians 8:9). This refers to the spiritual riches of forgiveness, adoption into God's family, and eternal life.

If you find yourself worried about your status or your needs, I encourage you to bring these concerns directly to God in prayer. Ask Him to reveal any areas where you might be trusting in money rather than Him, or where you might be failing to trust Him for your daily bread.

True security is never found in what we possess, but in who possesses us. If you are seeking peace and a right standing with God, know that it cannot be bought or earned. We are all spiritually bankrupt because of our sin, separated from a holy God. But God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. I invite you to turn from your sin today and trust in Jesus Christ for the forgiveness and life that only He can provide. What is it that prevents you from resting entirely in His provision for your soul?

question: Will the sun really be turned to darkness and the moon to blood (Joel 2:31)?
answer:
The Word of God is certain and every prophecy He has spoken will be fulfilled exactly as He intended. When the prophet Joel declares that the sun will be turned to darkness and the moon to blood, he is describing the terrifying and majestic signs that precede the great and awesome Day of the LORD (Joel 2:31).

Christians generally understand these celestial wonders in a few ways, as this falls into the category of eschatology where faithful believers may differ on the exact nature of the fulfillment. Some take a strictly literal view, believing that God will miraculously alter the light of the sun and the physical appearance of the moon through His divine power. Others suggest a phenomenological fulfillment, where cataclysmic events on earth, such as volcanic activity or the smoke of judgment, obscure the sky so that the sun is darkened and the moon takes on a blood red hue (Revelation 6:12).

Regardless of the specific physical mechanism, the truth remains that these signs are historical certainties yet to be fully realized. They serve as a final herald of God's direct intervention in human history. The Apostle Peter quoted this very passage on the Day of Pentecost to explain the pouring out of the Holy Spirit, noting that the "last days" had begun (Acts 2:17; Acts 2:20).

The ultimate purpose of such staggering signs is to lead the heart to repentance. While the Day of the Lord is a day of darkness and judgment for those who reject God, it is a day of ultimate redemption for His children. The very next verse provides the most beautiful promise in the midst of this cosmic shaking: "And everyone who calls on the name of the LORD will be saved" (Joel 2:32).

You may wonder if such a day is truly coming or if it feels too distant to matter. Yet, the holiness of God requires that He eventually deal with the sin and injustice of this world. We are all separated from God by our sin, but in His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. 

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you. He is the only refuge from the coming judgment. Have you ever considered what it means to call upon His name for your own salvation?

As you meditate on these things, I encourage you to read through the book of Joel and the book of Acts, chapter 2. Bring these verses before the Lord in prayer, asking Him to give you a heart that is ready for His return. It is also vital to find a local, Bible preaching church where you can grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord alongside other believers.

question: How can I overcome spiritual apathy?
answer:
Spiritual apathy is a state where the soul’s affection for God has grown cold, often resulting from the subtle encroachment of worldly cares or a neglect of the communal and private habits of grace. To overcome this "slumber of the soul," as some have called it, you must look away from your own flickering feelings and fix your gaze upon the unchanging Christ.

In the Christian life, we describe our growth in holiness as progressive sanctification. This is a work of the Holy Spirit, but it involves our active pursuit of God. Apathy often takes root when we treat the Gospel as a finished lesson rather than the very air we breathe. When the heart is cold, it is usually because it has wandered from the fires of the Cross.

To move from apathy to fervor, consider these biblical paths:

**Recall Your First Love**
The Lord Jesus cautioned the church in Ephesus because they had abandoned the love they had at first. He commanded them to remember from where they had fallen, repent, and do the works they did at first (Revelation 2:4-5). If you lack the "feeling" of devotion, begin with the "duty" of devotion. Often, the heart follows the feet. Do not wait for a surge of emotion to pray or read the Scriptures; rather, go to the Word to find the God who kindles emotion.

**Utilize the Means of Grace**
God has provided specific channels through which He pours His strengthening grace into our lives: the preaching of the Word, prayer, and the fellowship of the local church. Spiritual coldness thrives in isolation. We are commanded to stir up one another to love and good works, not neglecting to meet together (Hebrews 10:24-25). The warmth of other believers can often thaw a frozen heart.

**Mortification of Distraction**
We are told to lay aside every weight and the sin which clings so closely (Hebrews 12:1). Apathy is frequently the result of "spiritual indigestion," where the soul is so full of the world’s entertainment, news, and anxieties that there is no hunger left for God. Examine what is competing for your attention and be willing to cut back on secondary things to make room for the primary.

**Look to Christ’s Sufficiency**
Your standing before God does not depend on the heat of your affections today, but on the finished work of Jesus Christ. He lived a life of perfect zeal for you and died for your sins of coldness and indifference. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over death and the dullness of the human heart (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). When you feel you have no love for Him, remember His immense, sacrificial love for you (Romans 5:8).

**A Practical Step for Today**
Set aside fifteen minutes today specifically to read Psalm 51 and Psalm 63. As you read, make the words of the Psalmist your own petition, asking God, "Restore to me the joy of your salvation" (Psalm 51:12). Do not rely on your own strength to "fix" your heart, but ask the Holy Spirit to perform the work that only He can do.

If you find yourself questioning whether you truly know the Lord, remember that the Gospel is a gift for the weary and the heavy-laden. You can turn to God now, repent of your coldness, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What is it that makes the world seem more attractive than the Savior to you right now?

question: How can I experience healing and recovery after an abortion?
answer:
I hear the heavy burden you are carrying, and I want you to know that there is profound hope and restorative healing found in the person of Jesus Christ. The pain, guilt, and grief you may be feeling are real, but they do not have the final word over your life when you come to the fountain of God’s grace.

True healing begins by stepping out of the shadows and into the light of God's truth. While the world may try to minimize the significance of abortion, the soul often knows the weight of what has occurred because life is a sacred gift from our Creator. In the Bible, we see that God is both perfectly holy and infinitely merciful. He does not ask us to hide our brokenness or pretend our sins do not exist. Instead, He invites us to bring them to Him. Healing is found not by forgetting what happened, but by being forgiven for what happened.

The Lord Jesus Christ came into this world specifically to seek and to save those who are lost and burdened by the weight of their transgressions. On the cross, Jesus took the full penalty for sin upon Himself. This is the heart of the Gospel: God is holy, and our sin separates us from Him, but because of His great love, He sent His Son to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death in our place. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. His blood is sufficient to wash away every stain and to quiet the loudest conscience. As it says in 1 John 1:7, the blood of Jesus, His Son, cleanses us from all sin. Furthermore, 1 John 1:9 promises that if we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.

You do not have to earn your way back to God’s favor. Salvation and peace are gifts received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As you walk the path of recovery, I encourage you to take these practical steps:

First, speak honestly with God in prayer. Pour out your heart, your sorrow, and your regrets to Him, for He is near to the brokenhearted (Psalm 34:18). He already knows your heart, and He invites you to find rest in His finished work.

Second, do not walk this path alone. I urge you to seek out a faithful, Gospel-preaching local church where you can speak with a pastor or a mature sister in Christ. There are also many wonderful biblical counseling ministries specifically designed to help women and men navigate the unique grief of post-abortion recovery through the lens of Scripture. Bringing this secret into the light of a safe, Christ-centered community is a vital part of the sanctification and healing process.

Third, saturate your mind with the promises of Scripture. Remind yourself daily that in Christ, you are a new creation (2 Corinthians 5:17). When the enemy of our souls brings accusations against you, answer those thoughts with the truth of Romans 8:1, which declares that there is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.

What is it that feels most difficult for you to release to the Lord today? He is a patient and tender Shepherd who restores the souls of His people.

question: What does God mean when He says, “Seek me and live” (Amos 5:4)?
answer:
In Amos 5:4, the Lord issues a gracious command to the house of Israel to turn away from their empty, idolatrous religious rituals and return to a sincere, covenant relationship with Him. To "seek" God in this context means to pursue His presence, obey His commands, and desire His glory above all else, resulting in "life" which encompasses both physical preservation from coming judgment and the spiritual vitality found only in fellowship with the Creator.

At the time Amos wrote these words, the people of Israel were wealthy and outwardly religious, yet they were spiritually bankrupt. They frequented shrines in places like Bethel and Gilgal, but their hearts were far from God, evidenced by their oppression of the poor and their love for injustice. God’s call to "seek me and live" was a warning that their religious activities could not save them from the approaching shadows of judgment. True life is not found in a location or a ceremony, but in the person of God Himself.

This call requires a total turning of the heart, which the Bible calls repentance. Repentance is not merely feeling sorry for the consequences of sin, but a radical change of mind and direction where a person abandons self-rule and false gods to submit to the Lordship of Yahweh. As Amos later clarifies, seeking the Lord is inseparable from seeking what is good and hating what is evil (Amos 5:14-15).

However, we face a profound problem. Scripture teaches that in our fallen state, "no one understands, no one seeks for God" (Romans 3:11). Our hearts are naturally prone to wander and seek life in things that cannot satisfy. Because God is perfectly holy and just, our failure to seek Him deserves His righteous wrath. Yet, in His great love, God did not leave us in our deadness.

The ultimate fulfillment of the promise to "seek me and live" is found in Jesus Christ. Jesus is God manifest in the flesh, the one who came "to seek and to save the lost" (Luke 19:10). He lived the perfectly righteous life we failed to live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, defeating death so that all who trust in Him might truly live.

You can turn to God even now, repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of sins and the gift of eternal life. To live is to know Him, for as the Lord Jesus said, "this is eternal life, that they may know You, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom You have sent" (John 17:3).

As a practical next step in seeking Him, I encourage you to begin reading the Gospel of John in the Bible. Set aside a few moments each morning to ask God to reveal Himself to you through His Word, and find a local, Bible-believing church where the Gospel is faithfully preached so you can grow in fellowship with other believers. What is it that currently occupies the center of your heart and prevents you from seeking Him fully?

question: If aborted babies go to heaven, why is abortion wrong?
answer:
